sections.title: Definition of market infrastructure components
sections.summary: This section defines 'market infrastructure' as a licensed central counterparty, licensed central securities depository, licensed clearing house, licensed exchange, or licensed trade repository. It also provides definitions for 'participant', 'prescribed', 'Prudential Authority', 'Register', and 'registrar'. The definitions are updated to reflect specific regulatory frameworks and standards.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines key terms related to market infrastructure and regulatory entities, which may impact cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions by clarifying the scope of entities subject to data transfer requirements.

sections.title: Definitions of financial sector terms and related concepts
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used throughout the Financial Sector Regulation Act, including 'supervised entity', 'systemic event', 'systemically important financial institution', and 'taxable income'. It specifies that certain terms have the same meaning as defined in other acts, such as the Income Tax Act. The definitions establish foundational concepts for financial regulation and compliance within South Africa's financial sector framework.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions establish baseline terms for data transfers involving financial institutions, particularly clarifying what constitutes a 'systemically important financial institution' and 'systemic event' which could impact cross-border data flows during financial crises.

requirements.text: A 'supervised entity' means each of the following: A licensed financial institution; a person with whom a licensed financial institution has entered into an outsourcing arrangement; and a representative of a financial institution.
A 'systemic event' means an event or circumstance that may reasonably be expected to have a substantial adverse effect on the financial system or economic activity in the Republic.
A 'systemically important financial institution' means a financial institution that, if it fails, could cause significant disruption to the financial system.
Taxable income has the same meaning as defined in the Income Tax Act.

sections.heading: (1) In this Act ‘‘financial product'' means—
sections.title: Financial Sector Regulation Act alignment with other laws
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Financial Sector Regulation Act aligns with the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It requires that any provision of the Act that refers to an inspection must be interpreted as an investigation under the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It also specifies that references to the Authority publishing information online must be interpreted as publishing in the Register, and that prescribed fees must be determined under section 237 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies how the Financial Sector Regulation Act interacts with other regulatory frameworks, particularly regarding inspections, publications, and fees. It ensures consistency in regulatory procedures across different legal instruments.

requirements.text: A reference in this Act to an inspection must be read as a reference to an investigation in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to the Authority publishing information or a document on a web site must be read as a reference to the Authority publishing the information or document in the Register
The Authority may also publish the information or document on its web site
A reference in this Act to a prescribed fee must be read as a reference to the relevant fee determined in terms of section 237 and Chapter 16 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to a review of a decision of the Authority must be read as a reference to a reconsideration of the decision by the Tribunal in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act

sections.source_quote: A reference in this Act to an inspection in terms of a provision of this Act must be read as a reference to an investigation in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act.

sections.title: Amendment to cooperative financial institutions' regulatory framework
sections.summary: This section amends section 2 by substituting paragraphs (b) and (c) to establish requirements for cooperative banks and financial institutions. It mandates registration of deposit-taking co-ops, supervision mechanisms to protect members and the public, and the creation of a Development Agency for Co-operative Banks to enhance sustainability. These provisions apply to cooperative financial institutions operating within South Africa.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes regulatory requirements for cooperative financial institutions that impact data transfer through registration and supervision mechanisms. It requires specific safeguards for member data protection and public interest.

Establish supervisors to ensure appropriate oversight of cooperative financial institutions
Create a Development Agency for Co-operative Banks to enhance sustainability

sections.heading: (1) Subject to subsection (2), the responsible authority for a financial sector law is
sections.title: Licensing requirements for market infrastructure entities
sections.summary: This section specifies that entities must be licensed as exchanges, central securities depositories, trade repositories, clearing houses, or central counterparties to operate in financial markets. Unlicensed entities cannot purport to be such entities or use names suggesting connections to licensed market infrastructure. The section defines prohibited activities for unlicensed persons to prevent market manipulation and ensure regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes licensing requirements for market infrastructure entities, which affects cross-border data transfers involving financial market participants by restricting unlicensed entities from operating as market infrastructure providers. It requires clear licensing to prevent unauthorized data handling across borders.

requirements.text: A person who is not licensed as an exchange, central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house or central counterparty
A participant, authorised user, clearing member, approved nominee or issuer of listed securities

May not purport to be an exchange, central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house, central counterparty, external central counterparty, external trade repository, participant, authorised user or clearing member
May not behave in a manner or use a name or description suggesting connection to licensed market infrastructure entities

sections.source_quote: A person who is not— (a) licensed as an exchange, a central securities depository, a trade repository [or], a clearing house or a central counterparty; (b) a participant; (c) an authorised user; (d) a clearing member; (e) an approved nominee; [or] (f) an issuer of listed securities, may not purport to be an exchange, central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house, central counterparty, external central counterparty, external trade repository, participant, authorised user, clearing member, approved nominee or issuer of listed securities, as the case may be, or behave in a manner or use a name or description which suggests, signifies or implies that there is some connection between that person and an exchange, a central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house, central counterparty, external central counterparty, external trade repository, participant, authorised user or clearing member, as the case may be, where in fact no such connection exists.

sections.heading: (1) The object of this Act is to achieve a stable financial system that works in the
sections.title: Exemptions for market infrastructure entities
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions for exemptions from licensing requirements for market infrastructure entities. It specifies that exemptions must not conflict with public interest, frustrate the Act's objectives, cause financial hardship, or fail to meet equivalence criteria with foreign jurisdictions. The exemption process requires approval from the Financial Sector Regulation Authority, South African Reserve Bank, and Prudential Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs exemptions for market infrastructure entities, which impacts data transfers by requiring that exemptions do not cause financial hardship or conflict with public interest. It ensures that exempt entities meet equivalence standards for cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: Exemptions must not conflict with public interest
Exemptions must not frustrate the achievement of the objects of this Act
Exemptions must not cause financial or other hardship to applicants or clients
For external market infrastructure, applicant must be based in an equivalent jurisdiction with authorization from supervisory authority
Applicant must comply with joint standards for exemption
Applicant must cooperate and share information with the Authority, South African Reserve Bank and Prudential Authority

sections.source_quote: Exemptions must not— (aa) conflict with the public interest; or (bb) frustrate the achievement of the objects of this Act; and the application of the section will cause the applicant or clients of the applicant financial or other hardship or prejudice.

sections.title: Amendment of section 10 application procedures
sections.summary: This section amends section 10 to specify that applications for exchange licenses must be made in a prescribed manner and contain specific information. It requires the Financial Sector Regulator to publish application notices in two national newspapers and on their website, and to make proposed exchange rules and listing requirements publicly available for public comment.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires public disclosure of exchange license applications and proposed rules through official channels, impacting transparency in financial market data transfers and regulatory processes.

requirements.text: The [registrar] Authority must publish a notice of an application for an exchange licence in two national newspapers at the expense of the applicant
The [registrar] Authority must publish the proposed exchange rules and listing requirements on the Authority's website

The [registrar] Authority must provide a 14-day period for objections to proposed amendments

sections.title: Amendment of section 10 reporting obligations
sections.summary: This section amends section 10 to require that supervisory authorities must inform the Financial Sector Regulator of any matter that may pose systemic risk to financial markets as soon as they become aware of it. It also modifies the appointment of clearing houses or central counterparties for exchanges.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely reporting of systemic risk events to regulators, which impacts data transfer and risk management across financial markets.

sections.title: Amendment of section 12(6) listing restrictions
sections.summary: This section amends section 12(6) to specify that securities refused or removed from listing due to fraud, material misstatements, or non-disclosure cannot be listed by other exchanges for six months unless the refusal is withdrawn or reconsidered by the appeal board.
sections.dtia_summary: Imposes a six-month listing restriction on securities involved in fraud or material misstatements, impacting data transfer and market integrity.

requirements.text: No other exchange may grant inclusion of securities for six months if refusal was due to fraud or material misstatements

sections.source_quote: If the refusal to list securities was due to any fraud or other crime committed by the issuer, or any material misstatement of its financial position or non-disclosure of any material fact, no other exchange may, for a period of six months from the date referred to in paragraph (a), grant an application for the inclusion of the securities concerned in the list kept by it

sections.title: Substitution for section 50 on reporting requirements
sections.summary: This section substitutes section 50 to require cooperative banks, financial institutions, representative bodies, or support organizations to submit specific information or reports to the supervisor authority upon written notice. The reports must be prepared at the expense of the institution, ensuring accountability and transparency.

sections.summary: This section amends section 25(2) of the Financial Sector Regulation Act by substituting language to allow the Registrar Authority to prescribe standards for reports referenced in subsection (1). The amendment specifies that the Authority may prescribe standards for reports, including the conditions under which they must be issued. This applies to financial institutions regulated under the Act.

sections.title: Co-operative financial institutions: regulatory delegation and rule application
sections.summary: This section amends section 55(1)(f) to include co-operative financial institutions in the scope of regulatory delegation. It specifies that the Authority may delegate regulatory and supervisory functions to the Agency with Ministerial concurrence. The section also modifies rule application to co-operative financial institutions, allowing rules to apply generally or to specific institutions based on type or size.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines conditions for regulatory delegation to co-operative financial institutions, which may impact DTIA scope when assessing cross-border transfers involving such institutions.

Rules may be limited to specific co-operative institutions based on type or budgetary size

sections.title: Governor may direct financial regulators to share information with Reserve Bank
sections.summary: The Governor can direct financial sector regulators to provide specific information to the Reserve Bank for exercising powers under sections 14 or 15. Regulators must comply with directives to assist in systemic event management, including restructuring institutions, preventing risk spread, or increasing financial resilience. The National Credit Regulator must comply with directives after Minister consultation.
sections.dtia_summary: Directives require financial regulators to share data with the Reserve Bank during systemic events, necessitating DTIA for cross-border data transfers related to financial stability measures.

requirements.text: Direct financial sector regulator to provide information specified for Reserve Bank or Governor powers
Direct regulator to assist Reserve Bank in complying with section 15 during systemic events
Include directions to support restructuring, resolution or winding-up of financial institutions

Include directions to increase financial institutions' resilience to risks

National Credit Regulator must comply with directives after Minister consultation

sections.source_quote: The Governor may direct a financial sector regulator, in writing, to provide the Reserve Bank with information specified in the directive that the Reserve Bank or the Governor needs for exercising their powers in terms of section 14 or 15, that is in the possession of the financial sector regulator or obtainable by it.

sections.title: Central securities depository operational requirements
sections.summary: This section amends section 29 by requiring that a central securities depository licence specifies the registered office and operational locations. It also mandates that the depository cannot operate at any other location without joint prior written approval from the Registrar Authority, Prudential Authority, and South African Reserve Bank. This applies to central securities depositories operating in South Africa.
sections.authority: Registrar Authority, Prudential Authority, South African Reserve Bank

requirements.text: The licence must specify the registered office of the central securities depository in the Republic and the places where the central securities depository may be operated

The central securities depository may not be operated at any other place without the joint prior written approval of the Registrar Authority, the Prudential Authority and the South African Reserve Bank

sections.title: Systemic risk reporting obligations
sections.summary: This section amends section 30(2) by requiring that any entity must inform the Registrar Authority as soon as it becomes aware of matters that may pose systemic risk to financial markets. The entity must reasonably believe that the matter could increase systemic risk. This applies to financial market participants and institutions regulated under the Act.

requirements.text: Must inform the Registrar Authority as soon as it becomes aware of any matter that it reasonably believes may pose systemic risk to financial markets

sections.heading: The Financial Stability Oversight Committee has the following functions:
sections.title: Financial Stability Oversight Committee functions
sections.summary: The Financial Stability Oversight Committee serves as a forum for exchanging views on financial stability activities, makes recommendations on systemically important financial institutions, advises on financial stability risks and crisis management, and recommends steps for other state organs to assist in financial stability.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires coordination of financial data across regulators and the Reserve Bank during systemic events, necessitating DTIA for cross-border data transfers related to financial stability.

requirements.text: Serve as forum for representatives to exchange views on financial stability activities
Make recommendations on designation of systemically important financial institutions
Advise Minister and Reserve Bank on steps to promote financial stability
Advise Minister and Reserve Bank on crisis management and prevention matters
Make recommendations to other state organs on steps for financial stability

sections.source_quote: The Financial Stability Oversight Committee has the following functions: To serve as a forum for representatives of the Reserve Bank and of each of the financial sector regulators to be informed, and to exchange views, about the activities of the Reserve Bank and the financial sector regulators regarding financial stability;

sections.heading: (1) The Financial Stability Oversight Committee consists of the following
sections.title: Central securities depository participant approval rules
sections.summary: This section amends section 35 by requiring that central securities depositories must identify supervisory authorities and relevant laws for external participants or external central securities depositories. It mandates that the depository rules may provide for approval of external participants or depositories, subject to certain conditions. This applies to central securities depositories operating in South Africa.

requirements.text: The depository rules may provide for the approval of external participants or external central securities depositories to be participants of the central securities depository
The identification of the supervisory authority that supervises that external central securities depository

The identification of the relevant laws or depository rules that apply to each aspect of the participation, including, but not limited to, the laws regulating effectiveness against third parties and insolvency proceedings

sections.title: Central securities depository registration requirements
sections.summary: This section amends section 36 by requiring that securities held by a central securities depository must be registered in the name of the depository or its wholly owned subsidiary, unless they are bearer instruments, money market securities, or recorded in an uncertificated securities register in accordance with the Companies Act and depository rules. This applies to central securities depositories operating in South Africa.

requirements.text: Any securities held by a central securities depository in its central securities account must, unless they are bearer instruments, money market securities or recorded in an uncertificated securities register in accordance with section 50 of the Companies Act and the depository rules, be registered in the name of that central securities depository or its wholly owned subsidiary

sections.source_quote: 23. The amendment of section 36 by the substitution for subsection (1) of the following subsection: '‘(1) The [registrar] Authority may [direct] determine that any securities held by a central securities depository in its central securities account must, unless they are bearer instruments, money market securities or recorded in a uncertificated securities register in accordance with section 50 of the Companies Act and the depository rules, be registered in the name of that central securities depository or its wholly owned subsidiary, as defined in section 1 of the Companies Act, and approved by the [registrar] Authority.''

sections.title: Amendment to securities interest provisions
sections.summary: This section amends subsection (3) of section 39 to define when an interest in uncertificated securities may be granted. The interest must be effective against third parties in relation to a central securities account or securities account where it extends to all uncertificated securities standing to the credit at the time of the pledge. The provision applies to entities holding such accounts.
sections.dtia_summary: This amendment clarifies the conditions under which pledges of uncertificated securities can be effective against third parties, relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border transfers of securities interests.

requirements.text: An interest in respect of uncertificated securities may be granted where applicable and in the manner provided for in the depositary rules
The interest is effective against third parties in relation to a central securities account or securities account
The interest extends to all uncertificated securities standing to the credit of the relevant account at the time the pledge is effected

sections.source_quote: An interest in respect of uncertificated securities may be granted under this section, where applicable, and in the manner provided for in the depositary rules, and is effective against third parties, in relation to a central securities account or a securities account, where such an interest extends to all uncertificated securities standing to the credit of the relevant central securities account or securities account at the time the pledge is effected.

sections.heading: (1) The Governor must establish a forum called the Financial Sector Contingency
sections.title: Long title substitution for Financial Sector Regulation Act
sections.summary: This section substitutes the long title of the Financial Sector Regulation Act with a detailed description of its purpose, including promoting social and economic welfare through banking services, developing cooperative banks, establishing regulatory frameworks, and providing registration and supervision mechanisms for cooperative financial institutions.

requirements.text: The long title of the Financial Sector Regulation Act is substituted with a description of its purpose to promote social and economic welfare through banking services, develop cooperative banks, establish regulatory frameworks, and provide registration and supervision mechanisms

sections.source_quote: 25. The substitution for the long title of the Act for the following: 'To promote and advance the social and economic welfare of all South Africans by enhancing access to banking services under sustainable conditions; to promote the development of sustainable and responsible co-operative banks and co-operative financial institutions; to establish an appropriate regulatory framework and regulatory institutions for co-operative banks and co-operative financial institutions that protect members of co-operative banks and co-operative financial institutions; to provide for the registration of deposit-taking financial services co-operatives as co-operative banks and co-operative financial institutions; to provide for the regulation and supervision of co-operative banks and co-operative financial institutions; and to provide for the establishment [of co-operative banks supervisors and] a development agency for co-operative banks; and to provide for matters connected therewith'.

sections.heading: (1) The financial sector regulators must—
sections.title: Amendment to clearing house licensing requirements
sections.summary: This section amends multiple provisions of section 47 to update the requirements for clearing house and central counterparty licenses. It specifies that applications must include certain documents, fees, and rules that comply with specific sections of the Act. The amendments focus on the submission process and documentation for licensing.
sections.dtia_summary: This amendment details the documentation and submission requirements for clearing house and central counterparty licenses, relevant for DTIA when assessing the transfer of financial data across borders for such entities.

requirements.text: An application for a clearing house licence or central counterparty licence must be submitted
The application fee must be determined in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A copy of the proposed clearing house rules must comply with section 53

sections.source_quote: An application for a clearing house licence or central counterparty licence must be submitted, with the application fee determined in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act, and a copy of the proposed clearing house rules must comply with section 53.

sections.heading: An organ of state, other than a financial sector regulator, must—
sections.title: Amendment to clearing house licensing process
sections.summary: This section amends section 49 to specify the conditions under which the Registrar may grant a clearing house licence. The licence requires concurrence from the Prudential Authority and South African Reserve Bank, consideration of objections, and adherence to conditions set by the Registrar. The amendment focuses on the approval process for such licences.
sections.dtia_summary: This amendment outlines the approval process for clearing house licences, relevant for DTIA when assessing the transfer of financial data across borders for regulated entities.

requirements.text: The Registrar may grant a clearing house licence with concurrence of the Prudential Authority and South African Reserve Bank
The Registrar must consider any objection received as a result of the notice referred to in section 47(4)
The Registrar may impose conditions appropriate for the licence

sections.source_quote: The Registrar may, with the concurrence of the Prudential Authority and the South African Reserve Bank and after consideration of any objection received as a result of the notice referred to in section 47(4) and subject to the conditions which the Registrar may consider appropriate, grant a clearing house licence to perform the functions referred to in section 50, if—

sections.heading: (1) (a) The Governor may, by written notice to a financial institution, designate the
sections.title: Designation of systemically important financial institutions
sections.summary: The Governor may designate financial institutions as systemically important financial institutions through written notice. This power cannot be delegated. Before designation, the Governor must notify the Financial Stability Oversight Committee and invite the institution to submit comments. The Governor must consider factors including size, complexity, interconnectedness, substitutes, Committee recommendations, and submissions when making designations. Designations may be revoked and must be published.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the designation of systemically important financial institutions, which may impact cross-border data transfers for institutions subject to heightened scrutiny.

sections.heading: (1) To mitigate the risks that systemic events may occur, the Reserve Bank may,
sections.title: Prudential standards for systemically important institutions
sections.summary: The Reserve Bank may direct the Prudential Authority to impose prudential standards or regulator's directives on systemically important financial institutions related to solvency, leverage, liquidity, organizational structures, risk management, sectoral and geographical exposures, statistical returns, recovery planning, and other matters. The Prudential Authority must notify the Reserve Bank and Financial Stability Oversight Committee of enforcement actions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes prudential standards for systemically important institutions, which may affect cross-border data transfers through regulatory requirements.

requirements.text: Reserve Bank may direct Prudential Authority to impose prudential standards or regulator's directives on systemically important institutions
Prudential Authority must notify Reserve Bank and Financial Stability Oversight Committee of enforcement actions

sections.source_quote: 30. (1) To mitigate the risks that systemic events may occur, the Reserve Bank may, after consulting the Prudential Authority, direct the Prudential Authority to impose, either through prudential standards or regulator's directives, requirements applicable to one or more specific systemically important financial institutions or to such institutions generally in relation to any of the following matters: (a) Solvency measures and capital requirements, which may include requirements in relation to counter-cyclical capital buffers; (b) Leverage ratios; (c) Liquidity; (d) Organisational structures; (e) Risk management arrangements, including guarantee arrangements; (f) Sectoral and geographical exposures; (g) Required statistical returns; (h) Recovery and resolution planning; and (i) Any other matter in respect of which a prudential standard or regulator's directive may be made that is prescribed by Regulations made for this section on the recommendation of the Governor.

sections.heading: (1) None of the following steps may be taken in relation to a systemically
sections.title: Financial institution steps requiring Reserve Bank approval
sections.summary: This section mandates that certain actions by financial institutions require explicit approval from the Reserve Bank. These actions include the sale of personal data, disclosure of personal data to third parties, transfer of personal data, amalgamation or merger with other companies, and compromise arrangements with creditors. Without such approval, these steps are deemed void.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must obtain Reserve Bank approval before taking specific actions involving personal data transfers or structural changes, which impacts cross-border data transfers and institutional restructuring.

requirements.text: Go sekega, go fetola, go tlhabolola kgotsa go phimola laesense e e reboletsweng setheo seo sa ditsˇhelete
Go dirisa tharabololo e e kgethegileng go konotelela setheo sa ditsˇhelete ka boithaopo
Go dira kopo go kgotlatshekelo ya gore setheo sa ditsˇhelete se konotelelwe
Go thapa motsamaisi, motlhokomedi kgotsa motlhomamisi mo setheong sa ditsˇhelete
Go baya setheo sa ditsˇhelete ka fa tlase ga phaloso ya kgwebo kgotsa tiriso ya leano la phaloso ya kgwebo mo setheong sa ditsˇhelete
Entering into an agreement for amalgamation or merger of the financial institution with a company
Entering into a compromise arrangement with creditors of the financial institution
A step referred to in subsection (1) that is taken without the Reserve Bank's concurrence is void

(2) A step referred to in subsection (1) that is taken without the Reserve Bank's concurrence is void.

sections.heading: (1) An authority called the Prudential Authority is hereby established.
sections.title: Establishment of Prudential Authority
sections.summary: This section establishes the Prudential Authority as a juristic person operating under the Reserve Bank's administration. It clarifies that the Prudential Authority is not a public entity under the Public Finance Management Act, thereby defining its legal status and operational framework.
sections.dtia_summary: The Prudential Authority is established as a regulatory body under the Reserve Bank, which may influence data governance and oversight for financial institutions.

requirements.text: An authority called the Prudential Authority is hereby established
The Prudential Authority is a juristic person operating within the administration of the Reserve Bank
The Prudential Authority is not a public entity in terms of the Public Finance Management Act

sections.source_quote: 32. (1) An authority called the Prudential Authority is hereby established. (2) The Prudential Authority is a juristic person operating within the administration of the Reserve Bank. (3) The Prudential Authority is not a public entity in terms of the Public Finance Management Act.

sections.heading: The objective of the Prudential Authority is to—
sections.title: Objective of Prudential Authority
sections.summary: This section outlines the Prudential Authority's objectives, which include promoting financial institution safety and soundness, enhancing market infrastructure safety, protecting financial customers from institutional failures, and maintaining financial stability.
sections.dtia_summary: The Prudential Authority aims to ensure financial stability and safety, which indirectly affects data governance and risk management practices for financial institutions.

requirements.text: Promote and enhance the safety and soundness of financial institutions that provide financial products and securities services
Promote and enhance the safety and soundness of market infrastructures
Protect financial customers against the risk that those financial institutions may fail to meet their obligations
Assist in maintaining financial stability

sections.source_quote: 33. The objective of the Prudential Authority is to— (a) promote and enhance the safety and soundness of financial institutions that provide financial products and securities services; (b) promote and enhance the safety and soundness of market infrastructures; (c) protect financial customers against the risk that those financial institutions may fail to meet their obligations; and (d) assist in maintaining financial stability.

sections.heading: (1) In order to achieve its objective, the Prudential Authority must—
sections.title: Amendment of clearing house rules to align with financial regulations
sections.summary: This section amends subsection (1) of section 53 to require clearing house rules to be consistent with the Financial Sector Regulation Act, the Financial Sector Regulation Act, and any standards made under those acts. It also modifies subsection (2) to specify that clearing members must notify the clearing house immediately upon commencing or being subject to insolvency proceedings. Additionally, it mandates that clearing members must segregate and portability of funds and securities held as collateral, and that central counterparties must follow default procedures including close-out procedures in the event of a clearing member's default.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes specific requirements for clearing house rules to ensure consistency with financial regulations and insolvency procedures, which are critical for data transfer impact assessments involving financial market infrastructure.

requirements.text: Clearing house rules must be consistent with this Act, the Financial Sector Regulation Act and any standard made in terms of this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act
Clearing members must notify the clearing house as soon as it commences an insolvency proceeding or an insolvency proceeding is commenced against it
Clearing members must segregate and portability of funds and securities held as collateral
Central counterparties must follow default procedures including close-out procedures in the event of a clearing member's default

sections.source_quote: 34. The amendment of section 53— (a) by the substitution for subsection (1) of the following subsection: '‘(1) The clearing house rules must be consistent with this Act, the Financial Sector Regulation Act and any standard made in terms of this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act.''; (b) by the substitution in subsection (2) for paragraph (u) of the following paragraph: '‘(u) for the administration of securities and funds held for own account or on behalf of a client by a clearing member, including the settlement of unsettled transactions, under insolvency proceedings in respect of that clearing member; and''; (c) by the substitution in subsection (2) for paragraphs (z) and (aa) of the following paragraphs: '‘(z) for the segregation and portability of funds and securities held as collateral; [and] (aa) that clearing members must notify the clearing house as soon as it commences an insolvency proceeding or an insolvency proceeding is commenced against it; and''; (d) by the addition in subsection (2) of the following paragraph: '‘(bb) in the case of a central counterparty, for the default procedures to be followed, including close-out procedures, in the event of a default of a clearing member;''.

sections.heading: The Prudential Authority must manage its affairs in an efficient and effective way,
sections.title: Governance of Prudential Authority
sections.summary: This section requires the Prudential Authority to manage its affairs efficiently and effectively, establishing appropriate governance systems and processes aligned with internationally accepted standards and practices.
sections.dtia_summary: The Prudential Authority must implement robust governance systems to ensure efficient operations, which indirectly affects data governance and regulatory compliance for financial institutions.

requirements.text: The Prudential Authority must manage its affairs in an efficient and effective way
Establish and implement appropriate and effective governance systems and processes
Having regard to, among other things, internationally accepted standards and practices in these matters

sections.source_quote: 35. The Prudential Authority must manage its affairs in an efficient and effective way, and establish and implement appropriate and effective governance systems and processes, having regard to, among other things, internationally accepted standards and practices in these matters.

sections.heading: (1) The Governor must, with the concurrence of the Minister, appoint a Deputy
sections.title: Amendment of section 55 to define regulatory authority powers
sections.summary: This section amends section 55 to grant the Registrar Authority powers to require applicants to furnish additional information or verify it, take into consideration other information derived from various sources, and prescribe detailed requirements for licensing. It also establishes that requirements prescribed under this section continue in force immediately before the commencement of the amendment, allowing for the repeal of regulations and the prescription of new requirements in joint standards or conduct standards.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the regulatory authority's powers to require information and verify it, which impacts data transfer impact assessments by establishing conditions for data sharing and licensing.

requirements.text: The Registrar Authority may require an applicant to furnish such additional information, or require such information to be verified, as the Registrar may deem necessary

The Registrar Authority may take into consideration any other information regarding the applicant, derived from whatever source, including any other supervisory authority, if such information is disclosed to the applicant and the latter is given a reasonable opportunity to respond thereto
The Registrar Authority may prescribe any of the requirements referred to in subsection (1) in greater detail
Requirements prescribed under this section that are in force immediately before the commencement of this subsection continue to be in force
The Minister may repeal regulations, and new requirements may then be prescribed in joint standards or conduct standards
Paragraph (b) does not affect or limit the power of the Minister to prescribe or amend regulations in terms of subsection (1)(a)
Requirements prescribed in terms of subsection (1)(c) before the commencement of this subsection may be amended or repealed by conduct standards or joint standards

sections.source_quote: 36. sections.heading: The Prudential Committee must—
sections.title: Amendment of section 60 to align with Registrar Authority requirements
sections.summary: This section amends section 60 by requiring that financial entities comply with directives, requests, conditions, or requirements of the Registrar Authority in terms of financial sector laws. It specifies that the Registrar Authority may cancel or suspend a licence if an entity does not comply with these requirements or decisions of the appeal board.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that financial entities must comply with the Registrar Authority's directives and requests, which impacts data transfer and processing across borders by requiring adherence to regulatory conditions.

sections.title: Amendment of section 61 to regulate market infrastructure risks
sections.summary: This section amends section 61 to prohibit market infrastructure from conducting business that would create or increase systemic risk. It requires the Registrar Authority to make determinations on such risks after consultation with the Prudential Authority and South African Reserve Bank, with specific timelines for submissions and notifications.

sections.title: Delegation of powers and duties
sections.summary: The Prudential Committee may delegate powers to the Chief Executive Officer or staff members, with specific conditions. sections.heading: (1) A member of the Prudential Committee or of a subcommittee established in
sections.title: Disclosure of conflicts of interest in Prudential Committee meetings
sections.summary: Prudential Committee members must disclose any interest in matters being considered, including related parties' interests. Disclosure must be made promptly upon becoming aware of the interest. Members cannot participate in decisions affecting their disclosed interest unless they have disclosed it and other committee members determine it does not impair their functions. Failure to disclose voids decisions and requires reconsideration. Material interests must be disclosed, but certain interests like Reserve Bank officials or financial customers are exempt. Non-material interests do not require disclosure.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires disclosure of conflicts of interest for committee members involved in financial regulation decisions, impacting transparency in cross-border regulatory oversight and decision-making processes.

Staff must make timely disclosures of interests that could affect their functions

Non-pecuniary and indirect interests do not require special treatment
Reserve Bank officials and financial customers do not need to disclose their status
Non-material interests do not require disclosure
Failure to disclose material interests constitutes breach of duties and an offence
Prudential Committee must publish notice of disclosure failures on website
Chief Executive Officer must maintain disclosure and decision registers

sections.title: Amendment of section 71 for rule changes (alternative text)
sections.summary: This section amends section 71 to require that market infrastructure rules must not contradict regulations, conduct standards, prudential standards, or joint standards issued under this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It also outlines procedures for the Registrar Authority to amend rules, including publishing proposed amendments on the website and Gazette, and handling objections within 14 days.

sections.title: Staff members' prohibited conduct
sections.summary: Former or current Prudential Authority staff may not use their position or obtained information to improperly benefit themselves or others, impede the Authority's functions, or cause improper detriment to others. 'Benefit' and 'detriment' are not limited to financial aspects.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts staff from using regulatory data for personal gain or to undermine regulatory functions, impacting data handling and integrity.

It specifies that conduct standards are enforceable and apply to all relevant parties in the financial sector.

sections.title: CEO must provide regulatory information

sections.heading: (1) The Chief Executive Officer must—
sections.title: Prudential Authority record-keeping and reporting
sections.summary: The Chief Executive Officer must maintain full financial records for the Prudential Authority, prepare financial accounts for each financial year, and submit an annual report to the Minister within five months after the financial year ends. Financial accounts must reflect direct costs to the Authority and may be included as an annexure to the Reserve Bank's annual report.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes record-keeping requirements for financial data that impact data storage and reporting practices within the Prudential Authority.

requirements.text: Keep full and proper records of the financial affairs of the Prudential Authority

sections.source_quote: 55. (1) The Chief Executive Officer must— (a) ensure that full and proper records of the financial affairs of the Prudential Authority are kept and maintained; (b) prepare financial accounts for the Prudential Authority for each financial year which will form part of the annual report of the Reserve Bank; and (c) submit to the Minister, within five months after the end of each financial year, for tabling in the National Assembly an annual report on the activities of the Prudential Authority during that financial year, including particulars of any matters that may be prescribed by Regulation for this section. (2) The financial accounts of the Prudential Authority referred to in subsection (1)(b)— (a) must be disclosed in the annual report of the Reserve Bank in a manner that reflects the direct costs that accrue to the Prudential Authority; and (b) may be disclosed in the form of an annexure to the annual report of the Reserve Bank.

sections.title: Amendment of section 77 for insider definitions
sections.summary: This section amends section 77 to define 'inside information' as information that, if made public, would likely have a material effect on the price or value of securities listed on regulated markets or derivative instruments related to such securities. It also updates the definition of 'insider' to include directors, employees, or shareholders of issuers of securities listed on regulated markets.
sections.dtia_summary: Clarifies definitions of insider and inside information, which impacts data transfer assessments for insider trading and sensitive financial data handling.

requirements.text: Inside information is information that, if made public, would likely have a material effect on the price or value of securities listed on regulated markets or derivative instruments

sections.heading: The objective of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority is to—
sections.title: Amendment of section 78 for insider offenses
sections.summary: This section amends section 78 to define insider offenses, including the requirement that insiders must prove on a balance of probabilities that they disclosed inside information necessary for their employment functions, unrelated to securities trading, to avoid criminal liability.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines conditions under which insiders may avoid liability for disclosing inside information, impacting data transfer assessments for insider trading and sensitive financial data handling.

requirements.text: Insiders are not guilty of offenses if they prove on a balance of probabilities that disclosure was necessary for proper performance of employment functions unrelated to securities trading
Insiders who encourage or cause others to trade based on inside information commit an offense

sections.source_quote: An insider who knows that he or she has inside information and who encourages or causes another person to deal or discourages or stops another person from dealing in the securities listed on a regulated market or in derivative instruments related to such securities, to which the inside information relates or which are likely to be affected by it, commits an offence.

sections.title: Amendment of section 82 to specify claim submission procedures

sections.title: LiGatja ngekucocisana (Definition of LiGatja)
sections.summary: This section defines 'LiGatja' as a financial sector law that must be complied with by the Registrar Authority. It specifies that LiGatja refers to financial sector laws that are in force and applicable to the financial sector, including those that comply with directives and requests from the Registrar Authority or decisions of the appeal board.

sections.title: Executive Committee establishment and functions
sections.summary: The Executive Committee is established to oversee the Financial Sector Conduct Authority's management and administration. It has specific authority to authorize memorandums of understanding, delegate powers to the Prudential Authority, adopt regulatory strategies, and manage subcommittees. The Committee consists of the Commissioner and Deputy Commissioners.

sections.heading: (1) The Minister must appoint a person who is fit and proper and has appropriate
sections.title: Commissioner and Deputy Commissioner appointments
sections.summary: The Minister must appoint a Commissioner and Deputy Commissioners with appropriate financial sector expertise. Appointments require written agreement on performance measures and targets, and candidates must be fit and proper with no disqualifications or non-residence in the Republic.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the appointment process for regulatory officers which impacts data handling through personnel requirements and oversight.

Minister must appoint at least two, no more than four Deputy Commissioners
Commissioner and Deputy Commissioners serve in full-time executive capacity

If Commissioner unable to designate acting Commissioner, Minister may designate Deputy Commissioner
Person may not be appointed if disqualified or not ordinarily resident in Republic

sections.source_quote: 61. (1) The Minister must appoint a person who is fit and proper and has appropriate expertise in the financial sector as the Commissioner of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.

sections.heading: (1) The Commissioner—
sections.title: Commissioner's administrative responsibilities
sections.summary: The Commissioner manages the Financial Sector Conduct Authority's day-to-day operations and must carry out its functions, including exercising powers and duties, subject to section 60(3)(b). The Commissioner must implement policies and strategies adopted by the Executive Committee when acting in these roles. Deputy Commissioners' roles are determined by the Executive Committee.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the Commissioner's administrative duties and ensures alignment with Executive Committee policies, which may impact governance structures for data handling within the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.

requirements.text: The Commissioner is responsible for the day-to-day management and administration of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority
The Commissioner must perform the functions of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority, including exercising powers and carrying out duties associated with those functions
The Commissioner must implement the policies and strategies adopted by the Executive Committee when acting in terms of subsection (1) or (2)

sections.source_quote: 62. (1) The Commissioner— (a) is responsible for the day-to-day management and administration of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority; and (b) subject to section 60(3)(b), must perform the functions of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority, including exercising the powers and carrying out the duties associated with those functions.

sections.heading: (1) The Minister must, subject to due process, remove the Commissioner from
sections.title: Commissioner and Deputy Commissioner removal procedures
sections.summary: The Minister may remove the Commissioner from office if disqualified, with due process. The Commissioner may remove a Deputy Commissioner if disqualified, subject to Ministerial concurrence. Both may be suspended pending independent inquiry findings on performance, health, or misconduct. Absence from two consecutive Executive Committee meetings without leave constitutes material failure.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines removal and suspension procedures for Commissioners and Deputy Commissioners, which could affect data governance structures and accountability within the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.

requirements.text: The Minister must remove the Commissioner from office if the Commissioner becomes a disqualified person
The Commissioner must remove a Deputy Commissioner from office if the Deputy Commissioner becomes a disqualified person
The Minister may remove the Commissioner from office if an independent inquiry finds the Commissioner unable to perform duties due to health or other reasons
The Minister may remove the Commissioner from office if an independent inquiry finds the Commissioner failed in material performance against agreed measures
The Minister may remove the Commissioner from office if an independent inquiry finds the Commissioner failed in material discharge of responsibilities
The Minister may remove the Commissioner from office if an independent inquiry finds the Commissioner acted inconsistently with office duties
The Commissioner may remove a Deputy Commissioner from office if an independent inquiry finds the Deputy Commissioner unable to perform duties due to health or other reasons
The Commissioner may remove a Deputy Commissioner from office if an independent inquiry finds the Deputy Commissioner failed in material performance against agreed measures
The Commissioner may remove a Deputy Commissioner from office if an independent inquiry finds the Deputy Commissioner failed in material discharge of responsibilities
The Commissioner may remove a Deputy Commissioner from office if an independent inquiry finds the Deputy Commissioner acted inconsistently with office duties
The Commissioner or Deputy Commissioner must be taken to have failed in material discharge of responsibilities if absent from two consecutive Executive Committee meetings without leave

sections.source_quote: 65. (1) The Minister must, subject to due process, remove the Commissioner from office if the Commissioner becomes a disqualified person.

sections.heading: (1) (a) Komitikhuduthamaga e tshwanetse go kopana kgapetsakgapetsa ga go
sections.title: Executive Committee meeting procedures
sections.summary: The Executive Committee must hold meetings at times and places determined by the Commissioner, with a quorum of majority members. The Commissioner chairs meetings when present; otherwise, a Deputy Commissioner chairs. The Commissioner may invite representatives from other regulatory bodies but they cannot vote. Minutes must be kept in a manner determined by the Commissioner.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs Executive Committee meeting procedures, which may impact how data governance decisions are made and documented within the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.

requirements.text: Meetings of the Executive Committee must be held at times and places determined by the Commissioner
A quorum for Executive Committee meetings is a majority of its members
The Commissioner chairs Executive Committee meetings when present
If the Commissioner is not present, a Deputy Commissioner nominated by the Commissioner chairs meetings
The Commissioner may invite representatives from other regulatory bodies to attend Executive Committee meetings
Persons invited to Executive Committee meetings have no right to vote
The Commissioner must ensure minutes of each Executive Committee meeting are kept in a manner determined by the Commissioner

sections.source_quote: 66. (2) Meetings of the Executive Committee must be held at times and, except where subsection (1)(b) applies, at places determined by the Commissioner.

sections.heading: (1) The Director-General must establish—
sections.title: Subcommittee establishment for Financial Sector Conduct Authority
sections.summary: The Director-General must establish two subcommittees: one for reviewing remuneration policies and another for monitoring risks. Subcommittees may include non-Executive Committee members and non-staff members, with a majority not being staff members. Members hold office for periods and terms determined by the Director-General or Executive Committee.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes subcommittees for risk management and remuneration oversight, which could influence data governance practices within the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.

requirements.text: The Director-General must establish a subcommittee to review, monitor and advise the Executive Committee on the remuneration policy of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority

The Director-General must establish a subcommittee to review, monitor and advise the Executive Committee on the risks faced by the Financial Sector Conduct Authority and plans for managing those risks
The Executive Committee may establish one or more other subcommittees for the Financial Sector Conduct Authority
The majority of members of a subcommittee established in terms of subsection (1) may not be staff members of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority
A subcommittee established in terms of subsection (2) may include persons who are neither members of the Executive Committee nor staff members of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority
A disqualified person may not be or remain a member of a subcommittee
Members of subcommittees hold office for the period and terms determined by the Director-General or Executive Committee

sections.source_quote: 68. sections.heading: (1) The Commissioner, each Deputy Commissioner and each member of a
sections.title: Regulator's powers after on-site inspection
sections.summary: This section amends section 96 to grant the Financial Sector Regulation Authority powers to direct respondents to take steps or refrain from actions following on-site inspections or investigations. The Authority may require respondents to terminate or remedy irregularities disclosed during inspections, but cannot make orders under section 6D(2)(b) of the Financial Institutions (Protection of Funds) Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows regulators to issue directives after inspections, which may impact data transfers if the directives involve cross-border data handling. It requires remedial actions for irregularities, potentially affecting data storage and sharing.

requirements.text: After a supervisory on-site visit or inspection or an investigation has been conducted under section 95
The [registrar] Authority may, in order to achieve the objects of this Act referred to in section 2

Direct the respondent to take any steps, or to refrain from performing or continuing to perform any act, in order to terminate or remedy any irregularity or state of affairs disclosed by the supervisory on-site visit or investigation
Provided that the registrar may not make an order contemplated in section 6D(2)(b) of the Financial Institutions (Protection of Funds) Act

sections.source_quote: 69. The amendment of section 96— (a) by the substitution for the heading of the section of the following heading: 'Powers of [registrar] Authority after supervisory on-site [visit or] inspection or investigation'; (b) by the substitution for the words preceding paragraph (a) of the following words: 'After [an] a supervisory on-site [visit or] inspection or an investigation has been conducted [under section 95], the [registrar] Authority may, in order to achieve the objects of this Act referred to in section 2—'; and (c) by the substitution for paragraph (c) of the following paragraph: '(c) direct the respondent to take any steps, or to refrain from performing or continuing to perform any act, in order to terminate or remedy any irregularity or state of affairs disclosed by the supervisory on-site [visit or] inspection or investigation: Provided that the registrar may not make an order contemplated in section 6D(2)(b) of the Financial Institutions (Protection of Funds) Act.'

sections.heading: (1) The Executive Committee must, within six months after the date on which this
sections.title: Regulatory strategy must align with Financial Sector Conduct Authority's objectives
sections.summary: The regulatory strategy must ensure the Financial Sector Conduct Authority's regulatory and supervisory functions align with section 58, which likely relates to financial sector oversight. The strategy must define specific priorities, outcomes, and guiding principles for the Authority's operations, including transparency, consultation, and accountability mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that the regulatory strategy must align with section 58, which is critical for ensuring the Financial Sector Conduct Authority's oversight mechanisms are effective for data transfers and financial operations.

requirements.text: The regulatory strategy must be aimed at giving effect to section 58

sections.source_quote: 70. (3) The Executive Committee must review its regulatory strategy at least annually, and may amend it at any time.

sections.heading: (1) The Executive Committee may, in writing—
sections.title: Delegation of powers and duties to Commissioner or Deputy Commissioner
sections.summary: The Executive Committee may delegate powers and duties of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority to the Commissioner or Deputy Commissioner, except for specific powers like delegation itself or powers in section 60(3)(b). Delegations must be written, may be amended, and must not constrain the Authority's objectives. The Commissioner may delegate powers to Deputy Commissioners or staff members, and Deputy Commissioners may delegate to staff members.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows for delegation of powers and duties related to financial sector oversight, which impacts how data transfers and processing are managed by delegated entities within the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.

requirements.text: The Executive Committee may delegate any power or duty of, or delegated to, the Financial Sector Conduct Authority in terms of a financial sector law to the Commissioner or a Deputy Commissioner
The delegation must exclude the power to delegate contained in this subsection
The delegation must exclude the powers referred to in section 60(3)(b)(i) to (viii)
The Executive Committee may delegate to an administrative action committee the power to impose administrative penalties specified in the delegation
The Commissioner may delegate any power or duty assigned or delegated to the Commissioner in terms of a financial sector law to a Deputy Commissioner or a staff member
The Commissioner may delegate any power or duty to a staff member of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority
A Deputy Commissioner may delegate any power or duty delegated to that Deputy Commissioner in terms of a financial sector law to a staff member of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority
A delegation in terms of subsection (2)(a)(ii) or (3)(a) may be made to a specified person or to a person holding a specified position
Any power or duty of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority may be delegated to the Prudential Authority by a section 77 memorandum of understanding
A delegation made in terms of this section is subject to the limitations and conditions specified in the delegation
The delegation does not divest the Financial Sector Conduct Authority, the Commissioner or the Deputy Commissioner concerned of responsibility in respect of the delegated power or duty
A delegation may be revoked in writing at any time, but a revocation does not affect any rights or liabilities accrued because of the acts of the delegate

sections.source_quote: 71. (1) The Executive Committee may, in accordance with applicable law, delegate powers and duties to the Commissioner or Deputy Commissioner, except for specific powers.

sections.heading: (1) A member of the Executive Committee must disclose, at a meeting of the
sections.title: Disclosure of interests by Executive Committee members
sections.summary: Executive Committee members must disclose any interest in matters being considered by them, whether direct or indirect, and related parties. Disclosures must be made as soon as practicable after becoming aware of the interest. Members may not perform functions related to the matter unless they have disclosed the interest and other members have decided it does not affect their proper execution of functions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires disclosure of interests by Executive Committee members, which is critical for transparency in financial oversight and data handling processes.

requirements.text: A member of the Executive Committee must disclose any interest in any matter that is being or is intended to be considered by him or her
A disclosure must be given as soon as practicable after the member becomes aware of the interest
A member may not perform a function in relation to the matter unless they have disclosed the interest and other members have decided it does not affect proper execution of functions
Any consideration of a matter that does not comply with disclosure requirements is void and must be reconsidered
The Commissioner must maintain a register of all disclosures and decisions made in terms of this section

sections.source_quote: 72. (1) A member of the Executive Committee must disclose, at a meeting of the Executive Committee, or in writing to each of the other members, any interest in any matter that is being or is intended to be considered by him or her, whether or not at a meeting of the Executive Committee, being an interest that the member has or a person who is a related party to the member has.

sections.heading: (1) The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may, in accordance with applicable
sections.title: Financial Sector Conduct Authority's authority to appoint staff and enter contracts
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may appoint employees, enter secondment arrangements, engage contractors, enter contracts, acquire and dispose of property, insure itself, and do anything necessary for its functions. However, it cannot enter secondment arrangements or engage employees without written agreements on performance measures and expected performance levels.
sections.dtia_summary: This section grants the Financial Sector Conduct Authority broad authority to manage its staff and operations, which impacts how data transfers and processing are handled within the financial sector.

requirements.text: The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may appoint persons as employees
The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may enter into secondment arrangements
The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may engage persons on contract otherwise than as employees
The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may enter into contracts
The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may acquire and dispose of property
The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may insure itself against any loss, damage, risk or liability
The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may do anything else necessary for the performance of its functions
The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may not enter into a secondment arrangement unless the person and the Authority have agreed in writing on performance measures
The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may not enter into a secondment arrangement unless the person and the Authority have agreed in writing on the level of performance that must be achieved

sections.source_quote: 73. (1) The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may, in accordance with applicable law, appoint employees, enter into secondment arrangements, engage persons on contract, enter contracts, acquire and dispose of property, insure itself, and do anything necessary for its functions.

sections.heading: (1) A person who is or was a staff member of the Financial Sector Conduct
sections.title: Appeals against regulatory decisions
sections.summary: This section amends section 105 to allow persons aggrieved by decisions of the Financial Sector Regulation Authority or exchanges to appeal. The appeal process covers decisions related to authorizations, listings of securities, and participation in central securities depositories. The appeal mechanism is designed to address disputes with regulatory bodies.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes an appeal process for regulatory decisions, which may impact data transfers if the decisions involve cross-border financial entities. It provides a mechanism for resolving disputes over data handling practices.

requirements.text: A person aggrieved by a decision of the [registrar] Authority under a power conferred or a duty imposed upon the [registrar] Authority by or under this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act
An exchange to refuse an application by that person to be admitted as an authorised user
An exchange to withdraw the authorisation of an authorised user or to direct an authorised user to terminate the access to the exchange by an officer or employee of such authorised user
An exchange to defer, refuse or grant an application for the inclusion of securities in the list or to remove securities from the list or to suspend the trading in listed securities
A central securities depository to refuse an application by a person to be accepted as a participant

A central securities depository to terminate the participation of a participant or to direct a participant to terminate the access to the central securities depository by an officer or employee of a participant

sections.source_quote: 74. The amendment of section 105— (a) by the substitution for subsection (1) of the following subsection: '(1) A person aggrieved by a decision of— (a) the [registrar] Authority under a power conferred or a duty imposed upon the [registrar] Authority by or under this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act; (b) an exchange to refuse an application by that person to be admitted as an authorised user; (c) an exchange to withdraw the authorisation of an authorised user or to direct an authorised user to terminate the access to the exchange by an officer or employee of such authorised user; (d) an exchange to defer, refuse or grant an application for the inclusion of securities in the list or to remove securities from the list or to suspend the trading in listed securities; (e) a central securities depository to refuse an application by a person to be accepted as a participant; (f) a central securities depository to terminate the participation of a participant or to direct a participant to terminate the access to the central securities depository by an officer or employee of a participant;'

sections.heading: (1) The Commissioner must provide the Executive Committee and the National
sections.title: Amendment of fee determination authority in financial regulations
sections.summary: This section amends section 108 by substituting a subsection that allows the Authority to determine fees for matters under the Act. It specifies who must pay fees, the payment method, and interest for overdue fees. The Authority has discretion to set these fees within the framework of the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This amendment clarifies fee determination mechanisms but does not directly impact data transfer assessments as it pertains to fee structures rather than data flows.

requirements.text: The Authority may determine fees in respect of matters contemplated in this Act
The person by whom the fee must be paid is specified
The manner of payment of fees is specified
Interest payable in respect of overdue fees is specified

sections.source_quote: The Authority may determine fees in respect of matters contemplated in this Act and, in relation to those fees, the person by whom the fee must be paid, the manner of payment thereof and, where necessary, the interest payable in respect of overdue fees.

sections.heading: (1) The financial sector regulators and the Reserve Bank must co-operate and
sections.title: Regulators and Reserve Bank cooperation
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators and the Reserve Bank must cooperate and collaborate on financial sector laws, the National Credit Act, and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act. This includes sharing information, adopting consistent regulatory strategies, coordinating actions, minimizing duplication, and reporting annually to the Minister and Cabinet.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates collaboration between regulators and the Reserve Bank on financial data handling, which impacts cross-border data transfers through shared regulatory frameworks and information flows.
sections.authority: Financial Sector Regulators and Reserve Bank

generally assist and support each other in pursuing their objectives in terms of financial sector laws, the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act
inform each other about, and share information about, matters of common interest
strive to adopt consistent regulatory strategies, including addressing regulatory and supervisory challenges

co-ordinate, to the extent appropriate, actions in terms of financial sector laws, the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act, including in relation to standards and other regulatory instruments
co-ordinate, to the extent appropriate, actions in terms of financial sector laws, the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act, including in relation to licensing

co-ordinate, to the extent appropriate, actions in terms of financial sector laws, the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act, including in relation to information sharing

The financial sector regulators and the Reserve Bank must, at least annually as part of their annual reports, or on request, report to the Minister, the Cabinet member responsible for administering the National Credit Act and the National Assembly on measures taken to co-operate and collaborate with each other

sections.source_quote: 76. (1) The financial sector regulators and the Reserve Bank must co-operate and collaborate when performing their functions in terms of financial sector laws, the National Credit Act, and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act, and must for this purpose— (a) generally assist and support each other in pursuing their objectives in terms of financial sector laws, the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act; (b) inform each other about, and share information about, matters of common interest; (c) strive to adopt consistent regulatory strategies, including addressing regulatory and supervisory challenges; (d) co-ordinate, to the extent appropriate, actions in terms of financial sector laws, the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act, including in relation to— (i) standards and other regulatory instruments, including similar instruments provided for in terms of the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act; (ii) licensing; (iii) supervisory on-site inspections and investigations; (iv) actions to enforce financial sector laws, the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act; (v) information sharing; (vi) recovery and resolution; and (vii) reporting by financial institutions, including statutory reporting and data collection measures; (e) minimise the duplication of effort and expense, including by establishing and using, where appropriate, common or shared databases and other facilities; (f) agree on attendance at relevant international forums; and (g) develop, to the extent that is appropriate, consistent policy positions, including for the purpose of presentation and negotiation at relevant South African and international forums.

sections.title: Clearing house licensing requirements for central counterparties
sections.summary: This section amends section 110 by deleting subsection (5) and adding a new subsection (6). It mandates that clearing houses performing central counterparty functions must be licensed by the South African Reserve Bank and Prudential Authority by January 2022. The licensing process requires approval from the Authority, South African Reserve Bank, and Prudential Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes licensing requirements for clearing houses, which may impact data transfer assessments if they handle financial data across borders.
sections.authority: South African Reserve Bank, Prudential Authority

requirements.text: Clearing houses must be licensed as either an associated or independent clearing house by 31 December 2021
Clearing houses must be approved by the Authority, South African Reserve Bank, and Prudential Authority
Clearing houses must be licensed as both independent and central counterparty effective 1 January 2022

sections.source_quote: a clearing house performing the functions of a central counterparty must comply with any requirements imposed by regulations or standards, and must— until 31 December 2021, be licensed as either an associated clearing house or an independent clearing house, and be approved by the Authority, the South African Reserve Bank and the Prudential Authority

sections.heading: (1) An organ of state that has a regulatory or supervisory function in relation to
sections.title: False information in registration applications
sections.summary: This section prohibits providing untrue or misleading information in applications for registration as a co-operative bank or financial institution. The information must be accurate in material respects to avoid regulatory penalties.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures accurate registration details for co-operative financial institutions, which may affect DTIA compliance when verifying entity legitimacy across borders.

requirements.text: No person may provide untrue or misleading information in registration applications

sections.source_quote: It is an offence for any person in connection with an application for registration as a co-operative bank or a co-operative financial institution to provide any information that to the knowledge of such person is untrue or misleading in any material respect.
