sections.title: False information in registration applications
sections.summary: This section prohibits providing untrue or misleading information in applications for registration as a co-operative bank or financial institution. The information must be accurate in material respects to avoid regulatory penalties.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures accurate registration details for co-operative financial institutions, which may affect DTIA compliance when verifying entity legitimacy across borders.

requirements.text: No person may provide untrue or misleading information in registration applications

sections.source_quote: It is an offence for any person in connection with an application for registration as a co-operative bank or a co-operative financial institution to provide any information that to the knowledge of such person is untrue or misleading in any material respect.

sections.heading: (1) The Financial System Council of Regulators is hereby established.
sections.title: Fraudulent management of co-operative institutions
sections.summary: This section prohibits reckless management of co-operative banks or financial institutions with intent to defraud creditors. It also prohibits non-directors from benefiting from such management activities when conducted recklessly for fraudulent purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses fraudulent activities involving co-operative financial institutions, which may impact DTIA assessments when evaluating data transfer risks related to entity misconduct.

requirements.text: No person may manage co-operative banks or financial institutions recklessly with intent to defraud creditors
Non-directors may not benefit from reckless management of co-operative institutions for fraudulent purposes

sections.source_quote: It is an offence for any person to be involved in or take part in the management of a co-operative bank or co-operative financial institution while the business is carried on recklessly, with intent to defraud creditors...

sections.title: Amendments to financial sector regulation arrangements
sections.summary: This section amends multiple sections of the Act by inserting new items, substituting headings, and modifying existing items. It introduces a relationship between the Act and Financial Sector Regulation Act, criteria for external market infrastructure recognition, and licensing requirements for clearing houses and central counterparties.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restructures the regulatory framework for financial entities, which may affect data transfer assessments through licensing and recognition requirements for financial infrastructure.

requirements.text: Relationship between Act and Financial Sector Regulation Act is defined
Criteria for recognition of external market infrastructures are established
Withdrawal of recognition of external market infrastructures is defined
Principles of cooperation for external market infrastructures are established
Licensing of clearing house and central counterparty is defined

sections.title: Administrative justice compliance for decisions affecting rights
sections.summary: When an administrative decision or step by the Financial Sector Conduct Authority or Agency affects another person's rights, the authority must comply with the Promotion of Administrative Justice Act, 2000, unless a different fair administrative procedure is specified in this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act.

requirements.text: When an administrative decision or step by the Authority or Agency affects another person's rights, the Authority or Agency must comply with the Promotion of Administrative Justice Act, 2000

sections.heading: (1) The Financial Sector Inter-Ministerial Council is hereby established.
sections.title: Establishment of Financial Sector Inter-Ministerial Council
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Inter-Ministerial Council is established to facilitate cooperation and collaboration between Cabinet members responsible for financial sector regulation. Its members include the Minister and specific Cabinet members responsible for consumer protection, health, and economic development. The Council's objective is to provide a forum for discussing matters of common interest related to financial sector regulation.

requirements.text: The Financial Sector Inter-Ministerial Council is hereby established.

The objective of the Inter-Ministerial Council is to facilitate co-operation and collaboration between Cabinet members responsible for administering legislation relevant to the regulation and supervision of the financial sector by providing a forum for discussion and consideration of matters of common interest.

The members of the Inter-Ministerial Council are the Minister; the Cabinet members responsible for consumer protection and consumer credit matters; the Cabinet member responsible for health; and the Cabinet member responsible for economic development.

sections.source_quote: 83. (1) The Financial Sector Inter-Ministerial Council is hereby established. (2) The objective of the Inter-Ministerial Council is to facilitate co-operation and collaboration between Cabinet members responsible for administering legislation relevant to the regulation and supervision of the financial sector by providing a forum for discussion and consideration of matters of common interest. (3) The members of the Inter-Ministerial Council are— (a) the Minister; (b) the Cabinet members responsible for consumer protection and consumer credit matters; (c) the Cabinet member responsible for health; and (d) the Cabinet member responsible for economic development.

sections.title: Authority may establish conduct standards for market abuse monitoring
sections.summary: This section grants the Authority the power to establish conduct standards or regulator's directives for monitoring and identifying possible contraventions of the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It also allows the Authority to create conduct standards for the disclosure of inside information.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables the Authority to set standards for market abuse monitoring and inside information disclosure, which could influence data transfer practices related to financial market activities.

requirements.text: After consultation with relevant regulated markets, the Authority may make conduct standards for monitoring and identifying possible contraventions
The Authority may make conduct standards for the disclosure of inside information

sections.source_quote: 84. sections.title: Directorate of Market Abuse established under Financial Sector Regulation Act
sections.summary: This section establishes the Directorate of Market Abuse as the continuation of the Insider Trading Directorate from previous legislation. It specifies that the Authority may determine the functions, powers, and duties of the Directorate, including conducting investigations into offences under specified sections of the Act. The Directorate consists of members and alternate members appointed by the Authority, with specific industry representation requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates a regulatory body for market abuse oversight, which impacts data transfer practices through its authority to investigate financial misconduct and manage related data flows.

sections.heading: (1) (a) The Inter-Ministerial Council must, as soon as practicable following the
sections.title: Independent evaluation of cooperation mechanisms
sections.summary: The Inter-Ministerial Council must commission an independent evaluation of cooperative mechanisms between financial sector regulators, the Reserve Bank, Financial Intelligence Centre, Council for Medical Schemes, and Competition Commission within six months of the expiration of the period described in section 77(1). It must also conduct evaluations every two years thereafter to assess effectiveness. Evaluations must analyze memoranda of understanding, consultations, and compliance with relevant sections, and must be tabled in Parliament with a Council report.

requirements.text: The Inter-Ministerial Council must, as soon as practicable following the expiration of the six month period described in section 77(1), commission an independent evaluation of the establishment of co-operative and collaborative mechanisms between the financial sector regulators, the Reserve Bank, the Financial Intelligence Centre, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Competition Commission.

The Inter-Ministerial Council must, every two years after the initial independent evaluation referred to in paragraph (a), commission an independent evaluation of the effectiveness of co-operative and collaborative mechanisms between the financial sector regulators, the Reserve Bank, the Financial Intelligence Centre, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Competition Commission.

An evaluation in terms of this section must at least contain an analysis and evaluation of the memoranda of understanding required in terms of section 77, the outcome of any and all consultations in terms of section 78, and compliance with those sections.

The Inter-Ministerial Council may on its own initiative, or at the request of a financial sector regulator, at any time commission an independent evaluation of the effectiveness of co-operation and collaboration between the financial sector regulators, the Reserve Bank, the Financial Intelligence Centre, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Competition Commission.

When a financial sector regulator makes a request for an evaluation, the Inter-Ministerial Council must consider the request and the concerns raised in the request regarding the effectiveness of co-operation and collaboration, and, if the Council rejects the request, provide the financial sector regulator that made the request with the reasons for rejecting the request.

Any evaluation commissioned by the Inter-Ministerial Council in terms of this section must be tabled in Parliament immediately following the Council's consideration of the evaluation, and must be accompanied by a report from the Council on the evaluation's contents.

sections.source_quote: 86. (1) (a) The Inter-Ministerial Council must, as soon as practicable following the expiration of the six month period described in section 77(1), commission an independent evaluation of the establishment of co-operative and collaborative mechanisms between the financial sector regulators, the Reserve Bank, the Financial Intelligence Centre, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Competition Commission. (b) The Inter-Ministerial Council must, every two years after the initial independent evaluation referred to in paragraph (a), commission an independent evaluation of the effectiveness of co-operative and collaborative mechanisms between the financial sector regulators, the Reserve Bank, the Financial Intelligence Centre, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Competition Commission. (2) An evaluation in terms of this section must at least contain an analysis and evaluation of the memoranda of understanding required in terms of section 77, the outcome of any and all consultations in terms of section 78, and compliance with those sections. (3) The Inter-Ministerial Council may on its own initiative, or at the request of a financial sector regulator, at any time commission an independent evaluation of the effectiveness of co-operation and collaboration between the financial sector regulators, the Reserve Bank, the Financial Intelligence Centre, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Competition Commission. (4) When a financial sector regulator makes a request for an evaluation, the Inter-Ministerial Council must consider the request and the concerns raised in the request regarding the effectiveness of co-operation and collaboration, and, if the Council rejects the request, provide the financial sector regulator that made the request with the reasons for rejecting the request. (5) Any evaluation commissioned by the Inter-Ministerial Council in terms of this section must be tabled in Parliament immediately following the Council's consideration of the evaluation, and must be accompanied by a report from the Council on the evaluation's contents.

sections.heading: (1) A financial sector regulator may establish an administrative action committee
sections.title: Composition of administrative action committee members
sections.summary: A financial sector regulator can create an administrative action committee to advise on matters referred by itself. Committee members must include a retired judge or an attorney with at least 10 years legal experience. Additional members may include non-Prudential Committee or Executive Committee members. The chairperson must be appointed from this group. Disqualified individuals cannot join the committee.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines committee structure for regulatory oversight, relevant for ensuring independent governance in cross-border financial regulatory decisions.

requirements.text: A financial sector regulator may establish an administrative action committee
Committee members must include a retired judge or an attorney with at least 10 years relevant legal experience
Additional committee members may include persons not in the Prudential Committee or Executive Committee
A person referred to in subsection (2)(a) must be appointed as chairperson
Disqualified persons may not be appointed to or remain members

sections.source_quote: 87. (1) A financial sector regulator may establish an administrative action committee to consider and make recommendations to the financial sector regulator on matters that are referred to it by that financial sector regulator.

sections.title: Authority sharing financial information with regulators
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Regulation Authority may share information with specified entities including the Takeover Regulation Panel, South African Reserve Bank, Prudential Authority, Financial Intelligence Centre, National Treasury, and persons responsible for insider trading regulation. This applies to matters dealt with under the Financial Sector Regulation Act. The sharing is permitted without specific conditions but requires the information to relate to matters covered by the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the regulator to share financial information with other authorities, which may impact cross-border data transfers if the recipient entities operate internationally. It requires data sharing with specific regulatory bodies but does not impose direct data transfer restrictions.

sections.title: Convening and chairing of administrative action committee meetings
sections.summary: Committee meetings are convened by the chairperson and chaired by the chairperson or a designated member in their absence. The committee determines its own procedures but must follow the regulator's directions. The regulator must maintain written minutes of all meetings in a specified manner.

sections.summary: The Promotion of Administrative Justice Act applies to all administrative actions taken by financial sector regulators under this Act or specific financial sector laws. This ensures administrative actions follow legal standards for fairness and consistency.

sections.title: Public consultation before amending administrative procedures
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators must publish draft procedures or amendments on their website and seek public comments for at least 30 days before finalizing changes. They must also submit drafts to the Director-General and other regulators, and consider all public feedback received during the consultation period. Regulators must repeat this process if significant changes are made to the procedures.

sections.title: Investigation powers for financial misconduct
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Regulation Authority may investigate complaints or allegations of misconduct by securities service providers. If the Authority has reason to believe a contravention is occurring, it may direct the respondent to provide information, documents, or records reasonably required by the Authority. The Authority may also require the respondent to appear before it at a specified time and place.
sections.dtia_summary: This section grants the regulator authority to investigate financial misconduct, which may impact data transfers if the investigation involves cross-border data collection or sharing. It requires detailed records from respondents, potentially affecting data storage and sharing practices.

sections.title: Conditions for revoking regulatory decisions
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators can revoke decisions if the decision was made due to fraud, inaccurate information, or invalidity. Revocation must not adversely affect existing rights of others, and regulators must notify the affected person and allow at least 14 days for submissions before taking action. Regulators must also consider all submissions received during this period.

sections.heading: In this Part, ‘‘maker'', in relation to a regulatory instrument, means the person
sections.title: Definition of 'maker' for regulatory instruments
sections.summary: This section defines 'maker' as the person who proposes to create a regulatory instrument. The term is used throughout Part F of the Act to refer to entities responsible for drafting regulatory instruments. The definition establishes the scope of who can be considered a maker under the regulatory process.

requirements.text: In this Part, ‘‘maker’’ means the person that proposes to make the regulatory instrument

sections.source_quote: 97. In this Part, ‘‘maker’’, in relation to a regulatory instrument, means the person that proposes to make the regulatory instrument.

sections.heading: (1) A regulatory instrument must not be made unless the maker—
sections.title: Pre-consultation requirements for regulatory instruments
sections.summary: A regulatory instrument cannot be enacted without the maker first publishing a draft, explaining the need and intended operation, detailing expected impacts, and inviting submissions. Submissions must be received within six weeks. Financial sector regulators must share documents with other regulators and relevant bodies. This section establishes mandatory pre-consultation steps before regulatory instruments are finalized.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit public consultation before regulatory instruments are finalized, impacting data transfer processes through mandatory stakeholder engagement and transparency requirements.

requirements.text: has published—(i) a draft of the regulatory instrument

has published—(iii) a statement of the expected impact of the regulatory instrument
has published—(iv) a notice inviting submissions in relation to the regulatory instrument and stating where, how and by when submissions are to be made
has, once submissions referred to in paragraph (a)(iv) have been received and considered, submitted the regulatory instrument to Parliament in terms of section 103(1)
The period allowed for making submissions referred to in subsection (1)(a)(iv) must be at least six weeks

If the maker is a financial sector regulator, the maker must provide a copy of the documents referred to in that paragraph to the other financial sector regulator, the Reserve Bank, the National Credit Regulator, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Director-General

If the maker is a financial sector regulator, the maker must provide a copy of the documents referred to in that paragraph to market infrastructure that has the function of licensing providers of securities services

If the maker is the Ombud Council, the maker must provide a copy of the documents referred to in that subsection to the financial sector regulators, the Council for Medical Schemes, the National Credit Regulator and the Director-General

sections.source_quote: 98. (1) A regulatory instrument must not be made unless the maker—(a) has published—(i) a draft of the regulatory instrument; (ii) a statement explaining the need for and the intended operation of the regulatory instrument; (iii) a statement of the expected impact of the regulatory instrument; and (iv) a notice inviting submissions in relation to the regulatory instrument and stating where, how and by when submissions are to be made; and (b) has, once submissions referred to in paragraph (a)(iv) have been received and considered, submitted the regulatory instrument to Parliament in terms of section 103(1). (2) The period allowed for making submissions referred to in subsection (1)(a)(iv) must be at least six weeks.

sections.heading: If a maker of a regulatory instrument intends, whether or not as a result of a
sections.title: Repeating consultation process for substantially different instruments
sections.summary: If a maker intends to create a regulatory instrument that differs materially from the published draft, they must repeat the entire consultation process outlined in section 98. This ensures that any significant changes undergo the same public review and stakeholder engagement procedures as the initial proposal.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires repeating the full consultation process if regulatory instruments are substantially different from the initial draft, ensuring thorough stakeholder engagement before finalization.

requirements.text: If a maker of a regulatory instrument intends, whether or not as a result of a consultation process, to make a regulatory instrument in a materially different form from the draft regulatory instrument published in terms of section 98, the maker must, before making the regulatory instrument, repeat the process referred to in section 98

sections.source_quote: 99. If a maker of a regulatory instrument intends, whether or not as a result of a consultation process, to make a regulatory instrument in a materially different form from the draft regulatory instrument published in terms of section 98, the maker must, before making the regulatory instrument, repeat the process referred to in section 98.

sections.heading: (1) If the maker of a regulatory instrument determines that compliance with
sections.title: Urgent regulatory instruments with expedited consultation
sections.summary: When compliance with sections 98 or 99 would cause financial harm or prejudice customers, the maker must publish a draft, invite submissions within seven days, and submit the instrument to Parliament. The maker must also report on consultation outcomes within 30 days of enactment. This section provides a streamlined process for urgent regulatory changes without compromising stakeholder engagement.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for expedited regulatory instruments with shorter consultation periods (7 days) when financial harm is imminent, requiring immediate stakeholder engagement and reporting.

sections.title: Post-consultation submission and effective dates for instruments
sections.summary: The maker must consider all submissions received by the consultation deadline and parliamentary deliberations when deciding whether to enact a regulatory instrument. Once finalized, the instrument must be published in the Register and takes effect either immediately upon publication or on a later date specified in the instrument. This ensures transparency and timely implementation.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regulatory instruments to be published in the Register and specify effective dates, ensuring clarity on when data transfer processes become operational.

sections.source_quote: 102. (2) A regulatory instrument must be published in the Register after it is made. (3) A regulatory instrument comes into effect—(a) on the date the instrument is published in the Register; or (b) if the instrument provides that it comes into effect on a later date, on the later date.

sections.title: Regulatory instrument submission to Parliament for consultation

For instruments under section 100, submission to Parliament is required for at least seven days, whether in session or not, with documents from section 100(1)(a).
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires regulatory instruments to undergo parliamentary review before implementation, ensuring transparency and stakeholder input in financial regulation decisions.

sections.heading: (1) The Prudential Authority may make prudential standards for, or in respect
sections.title: Prudential standards for financial institutions
sections.summary: The Prudential Authority may establish prudential standards for financial institutions providing financial products or securities services, market infrastructures, and key persons. These standards must aim to ensure safety and soundness, reduce financial crime risks, and maintain financial stability. They can cover capital adequacy, liquidity, asset quality, and other matters necessary for achieving these goals.
sections.dtia_summary: Prudential standards set by the authority directly impact financial risk management and operational requirements for institutions handling customer data, influencing data transfer and storage practices.

They can cover product design, marketing, complaint resolution, and financial education programs.

sections.title: Regulatory standards for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators may establish standards on specific matters including fit and proper persons, governance, financial management, risk management, outsourcing, record-keeping, and reporting. These standards must align with the objectives in sections 33 and 57 of the Act and may be amended or revoked by the regulator.

sections.title: Reserve Bank concurrence for payment services
sections.summary: Financial Sector Conduct Authority cannot impose licensing requirements on payment service providers without Reserve Bank concurrence. Similarly, financial sector regulators cannot establish standards aimed at maintaining financial stability without Reserve Bank approval.

sections.title: Standard applicability and publication
sections.summary: Regulators may create different standards for various financial institution categories or circumstances. Standards must be published on the regulator's website and may apply to existing activities, transactions, policies and appointments.

sections.heading: (1) A person may not provide, as a business or part of a business, a financial
sections.title: Licensing requirements for financial activities
sections.summary: A person may not provide financial products, services or market infrastructure without a license under specific financial sector laws or this Act. Licensing applies to contractors only if a responsible authority requires it, and persons cannot describe themselves as licensed without actual authorization.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets licensing requirements for financial activities, relevant for assessing whether data transfers involve licensed entities.

requirements.text: A person may not provide financial products, services or market infrastructure without a license in terms of specific financial sector law, National Credit Act or National Payment System Act
A person may not provide financial products designated in section 2 or financial services designated in section 3 without a license under this Act
Subsections (1) and (2) only apply to contractors if a responsible authority specifically requires licensing
A person may not describe or hold itself out as licensed without actual authorization
A person may not permit another person to identify itself as licensed without actual authorization
A person whose license has been suspended or revoked is not licensed

sections.source_quote: 111. (1) A person may not provide, as a business or part of a business, a financial product, financial service or market infrastructure except— (a) in accordance with a licence in terms of a specific financial sector law, the National Credit Act or the National Payment System Act; or (b) if no specific financial sector law provides for such a licence, in accordance with a licence in terms of this Act.

sections.heading: (1) Bothati jo bo rwalang maikarabelo bo ka, ka kopo, aba laesense.
sections.title: Conditions for financial sector license applications
sections.summary: This section outlines the conditions that must be met for a financial institution to be considered for a license. It specifies that the applicant must have sufficient resources and capacity to comply with financial sector laws and that issuing the license must not be contrary to the interests of financial customers, the financial sector, or the public interest. The responsible authority must determine the application within three months, with possible extensions up to nine months.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes timeframes and conditions for license determination, relevant to DTIAs as it affects the timeline and scope of regulatory oversight for financial entities.

requirements.text: The responsible authority must determine the application within three months after the application is made
The responsible authority may extend the three-month period for one or more further periods, but the total period may not exceed nine months
The responsible authority must notify the applicant of the determination within the specified period

sections.source_quote: 116. (1) The responsible authority to which an application for a licence has been made must determine the application by— (a) granting the application and issuing a licence to the applicant; or (b) refusing the application and notifying the applicant accordingly.

sections.heading: The matters to be taken into account in relation to an application for a licence
sections.title: Matters to consider for financial license applications
sections.summary: This section lists the specific factors that the responsible authority must evaluate when assessing an application for a financial license. These include the objective of the authority, the applicant's financial resources, fit and proper person requirements, governance and risk management arrangements, and whether the application contains false or misleading statements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it defines the criteria for license applications, impacting how financial institutions must structure their data handling practices to meet regulatory expectations.

requirements.text: The objective of the responsible authority as set out in section 33 or 57
The financial and other resources of and available to the applicant
Fit and proper person requirements applicable to the applicant and any key person or significant owner
Governance and risk management arrangements of the applicant
Whether the applicant made a false or misleading statement in or in relation to the application

sections.source_quote: 115. The matters to be taken into account in relation to an application for a licence include— (a) the objective of the responsible authority as set out in section 33 or 57; (b) the financial and other resources of and available to the applicant; (c) fit and proper person requirements applicable to the applicant and to any key person or significant owner of the applicant; (d) the governance and risk management arrangements of the applicant; and (e) whether the applicant made a statement that is false or misleading, including by omission, in or in relation to the application.

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority to which an application for a licence has been made
sections.title: License determination and reporting obligations
sections.summary: This section details the process for the responsible authority to determine a license application, including the timeframes for decision-making and the conditions under which a license may be granted. It mandates that the authority must notify the applicant within three months, with possible extensions up to nine months, and requires that the applicant has sufficient resources and capacity to comply with financial sector laws.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is critical for DTIAs as it establishes the regulatory timeline and conditions for license determination, directly impacting how financial institutions must structure their data handling practices to meet compliance requirements.

requirements.text: The responsible authority must determine the application by granting the license or refusing the application
The responsible authority must notify the applicant within three months after the application is made
The responsible authority may extend the three-month period for one or more further periods, but the total period may not exceed nine months
The applicant must have sufficient resources and capacity to ensure compliance with financial sector laws
Issuing the license must not be contrary to the interests of financial customers, the financial sector, or the public interest

sections.source_quote: 116. (1) The responsible authority to which an application for a licence has been made must determine the application by— (a) granting the application and issuing a licence to the applicant; or (b) refusing the application and notifying the applicant accordingly. (2) The responsible authority may not grant a licence to an applicant unless satisfied that— (a) the applicant has or has available to it sufficient resources and capacity to ensure that it will comply with the requirements of financial sector laws in relation to the licence; and (b) issuing the licence to the applicant will not be contrary to the interests of financial customers, the financial sector or the public interest.

sections.heading: (1) A licensee must promptly report any of the following to the responsible
sections.title: Reporting contraventions to the responsible authority
sections.summary: This section requires licensees to promptly report to the responsible authority any contraventions of financial sector laws, regulatory directives, enforceable undertakings, court orders, or tribunal decisions. It also mandates reporting if the licensee becomes aware that information provided in the license application was false or misleading, and specifies that such reports are not admissible in criminal proceedings except for perjury.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it establishes reporting obligations for contraventions, impacting how financial institutions must monitor and report data handling practices across borders.

requirements.text: A licensee must promptly report contraventions of financial sector laws
A licensee must promptly report contraventions of regulatory directives
A licensee must promptly report contraventions of enforceable undertakings
A licensee must promptly report contraventions of court orders
A licensee must promptly report contraventions of tribunal decisions
A licensee must promptly report if the licensee has become aware that information given in the application was false or misleading
Information reported in this section is not admissible in evidence in criminal proceedings except for perjury

sections.source_quote: 117. (1) A licensee must promptly report any of the following to the responsible authority that issued the licence: (a) The fact that the licensee has contravened or is contravening, in a material way— (i) a financial sector law; (ii) a regulator's directive or a directive in terms of section 202; (iii) an enforceable undertaking; (iv) an order of a court made in terms of a financial sector law; or (v) a decision of the Tribunal; (b) the fact that the licensee has become aware that information given in connection with the application for the licence was false or misleading.

sections.title: License variations by responsible authority
sections.summary: This section allows the responsible authority to vary a license by notice to the licensee, provided that the variation assists in achieving the objective of the authority as set out in section 33 or 57. Variations may include removing, varying, or adding conditions to the license, or changing the categories of financial products, services, or customers to which the license relates.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it outlines the conditions under which license variations can occur, impacting how financial institutions must adapt their data handling practices to regulatory changes.

requirements.text: The responsible authority may vary the license by notice to the licensee if the variation assists in achieving the objective of the authority as set out in section 33 or 57

sections.title: Conditions for suspending a financial license
sections.summary: The responsible authority may suspend a financial license for up to 30 days if the licensee applies for suspension, contravenes material conditions of the license, fails to pay license fees for 30+ days, or violates foreign laws corresponding to financial sector laws. Suspension can be refused if it harms financial customers or undermines financial sector laws. The authority may later revoke the suspension.
sections.dtia_summary: Suspension conditions include material breaches of financial laws, unpaid license fees, and foreign law violations. These conditions affect cross-border data transfers if licensees operate internationally.

sections.title: Conditions for revoking a financial license
sections.summary: The responsible authority may revoke a financial license if the licensee applies for revocation, breaches material conditions of the license, or ceases to conduct licensed business. Revocation can be refused if it harms financial customers or undermines financial sector laws. Revocation takes effect immediately or on a later date specified in the notice.

sections.title: Conditions for resuming licensed activity after suspension or revocation
sections.summary: The responsible authority may allow a licensee to continue licensed activities after suspension or revocation, subject to conditions aimed at ensuring fair treatment of financial customers and orderly termination of activities. This activity does not constitute a breach of section 111 or 162.

sections.title: Procedures for license applications
sections.summary: The responsible authority may determine written procedures and requirements for license applications, including institutional form, business activities, financial capacity, fit and proper persons, and operational governance arrangements. sections.heading: This Part applies in relation to licences in terms of all financial sector laws.
sections.title: Licensing concurrence requirements for financial sector regulators
sections.summary: This section establishes that financial sector regulators must concurrence on licensing matters. It requires that any action involving licensing, varying, suspending or revoking a licence, or granting exemptions must have concurrence from other financial sector regulators. The Reserve Bank must also concurrence if the action affects systemically important financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires concurrence from multiple financial regulators for licensing actions affecting systemically important institutions, impacting cross-border data transfer where such institutions are involved.

sections.title: Regulatory concurrence for financial licensing actions
sections.summary: This section prohibits financial regulators from taking certain licensing actions without concurrence from other regulators. Specifically, issuing, varying, suspending, revoking licences, or granting exemptions under financial sector laws requires concurrence from other financial sector regulators. For systemically important financial institutions, the Reserve Bank must also provide concurrence.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates concurrence from multiple regulators for licensing actions involving systemically important institutions, which may affect data transfer where such institutions handle cross-border financial data.

sections.title: Supervisory on-site inspections: document access and examination
sections.summary: During inspections, regulators may request business documents from individuals, question persons on premises, and issue directives for document production. Document producers may examine copies during normal business hours under supervision.

sections.source_quote: 136. (1) (a) An investigator may, for the purposes of conducting an investigation, do any of the following: (i) By written notice, require any person who the investigator reasonably believes may be able to provide information relevant to the investigation to appear before the investigator, at a time and place specified in the notice, to be questioned by an investigator; (ii) by written notice, require any person who the investigator reasonably believes may be able to produce a document or item relevant to the investigation, to— (aa) produce the document or item to an investigator, at a time and place specified in the notice; or (bb) produce the document or item to an investigator, at a time and place specified in the notice, to be questioned by an investigator about the document or item; (iii) question a person who is complying with a notice in terms of subparagraph (i) or (ii)(bb); (iv) require a person being questioned as mentioned in subparagraph (i) or (ii)(bb) to make an oath or affirmation, and administer such an oath or affirmation; (v) examine, copy or make extracts from any document or item produced to an investigator as required in terms of this paragraph; (vi) take possession of, and retain, any document or item produced to an investigator as required in terms of this paragraph; and (vii) give a directive to a person present while the investigator is exercising powers in terms of this section, to facilitate the exercise of such powers.

sections.heading: (1) An investigator may, for the purposes of conducting an investigation, do any
sections.title: Investigator entry and search powers with consent or warrant
sections.summary: An investigator may enter premises for an investigation with prior consent from the person in control of the premises after informing them of the purpose and scope of entry and search, or with a warrant. If consent is granted, the investigator may search premises for evidence of financial law contraventions. The authority also permits entry without consent if authorized by a warrant or by a regulator head with specific conditions. The investigator must conduct searches discreetly and with minimal disturbance to respect constitutional rights.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines conditions for lawful entry and search of premises by investigators, which impacts data transfer DTIAs by establishing when cross-border data access is permissible without violating privacy or constitutional rights.

sections.source_quote: 137. (1) An investigator may, for the purposes of conducting an investigation, do any of the following: (a) Enter any premises— (i) with the prior consent of— (aa) in the case of a private residence, the person apparently in control of the business reasonably believed to be conducted at the private residence, and the occupant of the private residence or the part of the private residence to be entered; or (bb) in the case of any other premises, the person apparently in control of the premises, after informing that person that— (AA) granting consent will enable the investigator to enter the premises and for the investigator to subsequently search the premises as referred to in paragraph (b) or (c), and to do anything contemplated in subsection (6); and (BB) he or she is under no obligation to admit the investigator in the absence of a warrant; or (ii) without prior consent and without prior notice to any person— (aa) if the entry is authorised by a warrant; or (bb) with the prior authority of the head of a financial sector regulator or a senior staff member of the financial sector regulator delegated to perform the function, if the head of a financial sector regulator or senior staff member on reasonable grounds believes that— (AA) a warrant will be issued under section 138(1) if applied for; (BB) the delay in obtaining the warrant is likely to defeat the purpose for which entry of the premises is sought; and (CC) it is necessary to enter the premises to conduct the investigation and search the premises as referred to in paragraph (b) or (c), and to do anything contemplated in subsection (6); (b) if the investigation is one referred to in section 135(1)(a), search the premises for evidence of a contravention of a financial sector law; or (c) if the investigation is one referred to in section 135(1)(b), search the premises pursuant to the request, subject to section 251.

sections.title: Warrant requirements for investigator entry
sections.summary: A warrant must be issued by a judge or magistrate for entry into premises without consent. The warrant requires specific conditions to be met, including that the investigation is necessary for the purpose of the entry, and that the entry is likely to yield information pertaining to the contravention. The warrant must be signed by the issuing judge or magistrate.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the legal framework for warrants that may be required for cross-border data access, ensuring that investigations comply with judicial oversight and specific conditions to protect data subject rights.

sections.heading: (1) (a) A person who is questioned, or required to produce a document or
sections.title: Right to object to incriminating information during investigations
sections.summary: A person questioned or required to produce documents during a financial regulator investigation may object to answering questions or producing documents if the answer or document may tend to incriminate them. The official conducting the investigation may require the person to answer or produce the document despite the objection. Incriminating answers or documents are not admissible in criminal proceedings except for perjury or specific contraventions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section protects data subjects from self-incrimination during investigations, which is critical for DTIAs to ensure compliance with data subject rights when transferring or processing data across borders.

sections.heading: (1) A person may give a written undertaking to the responsible authority
sections.title: Enforceable undertakings for financial sector compliance
sections.summary: The responsible authority may accept enforceable undertakings from financial institutions to comply with financial sector laws. If an institution breaches the undertaking, the authority may suspend its license. The authority must publish all undertakings, variations, and withdrawals. The Tribunal can issue orders to enforce compliance, including remedial actions for past contraventions and future conduct. Orders can be enforced as civil judgments if not complied with.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes mechanisms for enforcing compliance through enforceable undertakings, with consequences for breaches including license suspension and court orders. It impacts DTIAs by requiring assessment of contractual obligations and enforcement pathways for cross-border financial compliance.

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority for a financial sector law may commence
sections.title: Court proceedings for financial sector compliance
sections.summary: The responsible authority may initiate court proceedings against individuals for non-compliance with financial sector laws. The High Court can issue orders to ensure compliance, including for conduct that contravenes laws, past violations, or potential harm. The authority must publish court orders (excluding interlocutory orders). The court may not require the authority to provide damages in connection with the application.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables court enforcement mechanisms for financial sector compliance, relevant to DTIAs through potential cross-border court orders and publication requirements for financial institutions.

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority for a financial sector law may make a debarment
sections.title: Debarment orders for financial sector violations
sections.summary: The responsible authority may issue debarment orders against natural persons who have materially contravened financial sector laws, enforceable undertakings, or foreign laws. Debarment prohibits specified financial activities, acting as key persons, or providing services to financial institutions for the duration of the order. The authority must publish debarment orders and related orders. Debarred persons cannot engage in conduct that contravenes the order, including arrangements with others to do so.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates debarment mechanisms that impact DTIAs by restricting cross-border financial activities and requiring assessments of debarment effects on data transfers and processing.

The responsible authority must publish debarment orders and related orders

sections.title: Consultation requirements for debarment orders

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority for a financial sector law may, in exchange for a
sections.title: Leniency agreements for financial sector violations
sections.summary: The responsible authority may enter leniency agreements with persons who cooperate in investigations or proceedings for financial sector law violations. These agreements may exempt the person from administrative penalties if they provide full cooperation. The authority must publish leniency agreements unless publication would create safety risks or prejudice investigations. The authority may terminate agreements if the person provides false information, fails to comply, or agrees to termination.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides leniency mechanisms that impact DTIAs by allowing reduced penalties for cooperative behavior, relevant to cross-border financial compliance.

sections.heading: (1) Subject to subsections (3) and (4), a person is a significant owner of a
sections.title: Definition of significant owner of financial institution
sections.summary: A person is a significant owner of a financial institution if they, directly or indirectly, alone or together with related or inter-related persons, have the ability to control or influence materially the business or strategy of the financial institution. This ability can be demonstrated by holding 15% of the governing body appointments, requiring consent for such appointments, or holding a qualifying stake in the institution.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines who qualifies as a significant owner for DTIA purposes, impacting cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: A person is a significant owner of a financial institution if the person, directly or indirectly, alone or together with a related or inter-related person, has the ability to control or influence materially the business or strategy of the financial institution.

The person has the ability referred to in subsection (1) if the person, directly or indirectly, alone or together with related or inter-related person, has the power to appoint 15% of the members of the governing body of the financial institution.

The person has the ability referred to in subsection (1) if the consent of the person, alone or together with related or inter-related person, is required for the appointment of 15% of the members of a governing body of the financial institution.

The person has the ability referred to in subsection (1) if the person, directly or indirectly, alone or together with related or inter-related person, holds a qualifying stake in the financial institution.

sections.heading: (1) For the purposes of this section, a financial institution refers only to—
sections.title: Regulations for significant owners of financial institutions
sections.summary: Significant owners must obtain prior written approval from the responsible authority for financial sector law before effecting arrangements that change their ability to control or influence the business or strategy of a financial institution. This includes arrangements that increase or decrease their control, or result in them ceasing to be a significant owner. The authority must ensure the arrangement does not prejudicially affect the financial institution's prudent management and soundness.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulates arrangements by significant owners that could impact cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: A person may not effect any arrangement that will result in the person, alone or together with related or inter-related person, increasing or decreasing the extent of the ability to control or influence materially the business or strategy of the financial institution without prior written approval from the responsible authority for financial sector law.
A person may not effect any arrangement that will result in the person ceasing to be a significant owner without prior notification to the responsible authority for financial sector law.
The responsible authority must be satisfied that the arrangement will not prejudicially affect the prudent management and financial soundness of the financial institution.
The responsible authority must be satisfied that the person meets and is reasonably likely to continue to meet applicable fit and proper person requirements.

sections.heading: (1) The Prudential Authority may designate members of a group of companies as
sections.title: Designation of financial conglomerates by Prudential Authority
sections.summary: The Prudential Authority may designate members of a group of companies as a financial conglomerate, which must include an eligible financial institution and its holding company. Designation must be for prudential supervision purposes and requires prior notice and submissions from the holding company. The Authority must consult the Financial Sector Conduct Authority and consider risks to supervision, submissions, and prescribed matters. Designation may be exempted if compliance with notice procedures would cause material prejudice to financial customers or financial stability.
sections.dtia_summary: Designation of financial conglomerates requires specific notice procedures, risk assessments, and consultation with the Financial Sector Conduct Authority. This affects cross-border data flows within conglomerates by mandating structured oversight mechanisms.

sections.title: Prudential standards for financial conglomerate holding companies
sections.summary: This section grants the Prudential Authority the power to establish prudential standards that must be followed by holding companies of financial conglomerates. These standards can cover financial exposures within conglomerates, governance structures, reporting requirements for non-financial institutions, and risk management related to the conglomerate's financial soundness. The Financial Sector Conduct Authority also has similar powers regarding conduct standards.

sections.title: Prudential directives for financial conglomerate risk management

Directives may include restructuring requirements that must be approved by the Authority, with specific considerations for effective supervision and necessary restructuring. The Financial Sector Conduct Authority also has similar powers regarding compliance with financial laws.

The Authority must notify holding companies of proposed refusals with specific reasons and allow for submissions.

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority for a financial sector law may, by order served on
sections.title: Administrative penalties for financial sector violations
sections.summary: This section empowers responsible authorities to impose administrative penalties on persons who contravene financial sector laws or enforceable undertakings. Penalties must consider deterrence, cooperation with regulators, severity of contravention, financial impact, previous violations, effects on financial stability, and intent behind the conduct. Penalties may include reimbursement for reasonable costs and cannot be imposed if a criminal prosecution has already commenced.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes penalty mechanisms that impact data transfer assessments by requiring thorough risk evaluations for violations involving cross-border data transfers.

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority that makes an administrative penalty order may file
sections.title: Filing administrative penalty orders with court
sections.summary: The responsible authority that issues administrative penalty orders may file a certified copy of the order with a competent court if the penalty amount remains unpaid and no reconsideration or judicial review applications have been made within the prescribed timeframe. The filed order functions as a civil judgment and can be enforced as if issued by that court.

requirements.text: The responsible authority that makes an administrative penalty order may file with the registrar of a competent court a certified copy of the order
The amount payable in terms of the order has not been paid as required by the order

No application for reconsideration of the order in terms of a financial sector law or for judicial review in terms of the Promotion of Administrative Justice Act has been lodged by the end of the period for making such applications
If such an application has been made, proceedings on the application have been finally disposed of
The order, on being filed, has the effect of a civil judgment and may be enforced as if lawfully given in that court

sections.source_quote: 170. (1) The responsible authority that makes an administrative penalty order may file with the registrar of a competent court a certified copy of the order if— (a) the amount payable in terms of the order has not been paid as required by the order; and (b) either— (i) no application for reconsideration of the order in terms of a financial sector law, or for judicial review in terms of the Promotion of Administrative Justice Act of the Tribunal's decision, has been lodged by the end of the period for making such applications; or (ii) if such an application has been made, proceedings on the application have been finally disposed of.

sections.heading: All amounts recovered by a responsible authority as administrative penalties
sections.title: Application of administrative penalty funds
sections.summary: All administrative penalties collected by responsible authorities must first reimburse their reasonable costs and expenses related to enforcing the penalty order, and then the remaining funds must be transferred to the National Revenue Fund. This ensures proper financial accountability and public fund management.

requirements.text: All amounts recovered by a responsible authority as administrative penalties must be applied first to reimburse the responsible authority for its costs and expenses reasonably and properly incurred in connection with the relevant contravention, making the order and enforcing it
The balance after applying the amount in accordance with paragraph (a) must be paid into the National Revenue Fund

sections.source_quote: 171. All amounts recovered by a responsible authority as administrative penalties must be applied— (a) first, to reimburse the responsible authority for its costs and expenses reasonably and properly incurred in connection with the relevant contravention, making the order and enforcing it; and (b) then, the balance after applying the amount in accordance with paragraph (a) must be paid into the National Revenue Fund.

sections.heading: When determining the sentence to impose on a person convicted of an offence in
sections.title: Administrative penalties considered in sentencing
sections.summary: Courts must consider any administrative penalty order made in respect of the same set of facts when determining sentences for individuals convicted of financial sector law offenses. This ensures penalties are factored into judicial outcomes.

requirements.text: When determining the sentence to impose on a person convicted of an offence in terms of a financial sector law, a court must take into account any administrative penalty order made in respect of the same set of facts

sections.source_quote: 172. When determining the sentence to impose on a person convicted of an offence in terms of a financial sector law, a court must take into account any administrative penalty order made in respect of the same set of facts.

sections.heading: (1) The Ombud Council is hereby established.
sections.title: Establishment of the Ombud Council
sections.summary: The Ombud Council is established as a national public entity with juristic person status. It functions as the accounting authority for its own financial management under the Public Finance Management Act, ensuring transparency in its operations.

requirements.text: The Ombud Council is hereby established
The Ombud Council is a juristic person
The Ombud Council is a national public entity for the purposes of the Public Finance Management Act
The Chairperson of the Ombud Council is the accounting authority of the Ombud Council for the purposes of the Public Finance Management Act

sections.source_quote: 175. (1) The Ombud Council is hereby established. (2) The Ombud Council is a juristic person. (3) The Ombud Council is a national public entity for the purposes of the Public Finance Management Act, and notwithstanding section 49(2) of the Public Finance Management Act, the Chairperson of the Ombud Council is the accounting authority of the Ombud Council for the purposes of that Act.

sections.heading: The objective of the Ombud Council is to assist in ensuring that financial
sections.title: Ombud Council's objective for financial customers
sections.summary: The Ombud Council aims to ensure financial customers have access to affordable, effective, independent, and fair alternative dispute resolution processes for complaints about financial institutions and market infrastructures. This supports financial inclusion and customer protection.

requirements.text: The objective of the Ombud Council is to assist in ensuring that financial customers have access to, and are able to use, affordable, effective, independent and fair alternative dispute resolution processes for complaints about financial institutions in relation to financial products, financial services, and services provided by market infrastructures

sections.source_quote: 176. The objective of the Ombud Council is to assist in ensuring that financial customers have access to, and are able to use, affordable, effective, independent and fair alternative dispute resolution processes for complaints about financial institutions in relation to financial products, financial services, and services provided by market infrastructures.

sections.heading: (1) In order to achieve its objective, the Ombud Council must—
sections.title: Ombud Council's functions and responsibilities
sections.summary: The Ombud Council must recognize industry ombud schemes, promote cooperation between ombuds, protect their independence, raise public awareness, facilitate customer access, publicize schemes, resolve jurisdictional overlaps, monitor performance, and support financial inclusion. It must act without fear, favor, or prejudice.

requirements.text: The Ombud Council must recognise, in accordance with this Chapter, industry ombud schemes
The Ombud Council must promote co-operation between, and co-ordination of, the activities of ombuds
The Ombud Council must strive to protect the independence and impartiality of ombuds
The Ombud Council must promote public awareness of ombuds and ombud schemes and the services they provide
The Ombud Council must take steps to facilitate access by financial customers to appropriate ombuds
The Ombud Council must publicise ombud schemes, including publicising the kinds of complaints that different ombud schemes deal with
The Ombud Council must resolve, in accordance with this Act, overlaps of the jurisdictional coverage of different ombud schemes
The Ombud Council must monitor the performance of ombud schemes, including the extent to which they comply with the requirements of this Chapter and specific financial sector laws
The Ombud Council must support financial inclusion
The Ombud Council must perform any other function conferred on it in terms of any other provision of this Act or other applicable legislation
The Ombud Council may do anything else reasonably necessary to achieve its objective
The Ombud Council must perform its functions without fear, favour or prejudice

sections.source_quote: 177. (1) In order to achieve its objective, the Ombud Council must— (a) recognise, in accordance with this Chapter, industry ombud schemes; (b) promote co-operation between, and co-ordination of, the activities of ombuds; (c) strive to protect the independence and impartiality of ombuds; (d) promote public awareness of ombuds and ombud schemes and the services they provide; (e) take steps to facilitate access by financial customers to appropriate ombuds; (f) publicise ombud schemes, including publicising the kinds of complaints that different ombud schemes deal with; (g) resolve, in accordance with this Act, overlaps of the jurisdictional coverage of different ombud schemes; (h) monitor the performance of ombud schemes, including the extent to which they comply with the requirements of this Chapter and specific financial sector laws; and (i) support financial inclusion.

sections.heading: The Ombud Council must—
sections.title: Ombud Council governance requirements
sections.summary: The Ombud Council must manage its affairs efficiently and effectively. It must also establish and implement appropriate and effective governance systems and processes to ensure proper oversight and accountability. These requirements apply to the Ombud Council itself.

requirements.text: Manage its affairs in an efficient and effective way
Establish and implement appropriate and effective governance systems and processes

sections.source_quote: 178. The Ombud Council must— (a) manage its affairs in an efficient and effective way; and (b) establish and implement appropriate and effective governance systems and processes.

sections.heading: (1) The members of the Board are appointed by the Minister.
sections.title: Board appointments and eligibility
sections.summary: The Minister appoints Board members, including a Chairperson and Deputy Chairperson. The Commissioner and Chief Ombud cannot be Chairperson or Deputy Chairperson. The Deputy Chairperson acts as Chairperson when the Chairperson is absent. Board members must not be ombuds, staff of ombud schemes, or engaged in financial services, among other disqualifications.

requirements.text: The Minister must appoint a member as Chairperson and another member as Deputy Chairperson
The Commissioner and the Chief Ombud may not be appointed as Chairperson or Deputy Chairperson
The Deputy Chairperson acts as Chairperson when the Chairperson is absent from office or is otherwise unable to perform his or her functions
A person may not be appointed to, or hold office as, a member of the Board if the person is an ombud
A person may not be appointed to, or hold office as, a member of the Board if the person is a member of the governing body or staff of an ombud scheme
A person may not be appointed to, or hold office as, a member of the Board if the person is a member of the staff of the Ombud Council
A person may not be appointed to, or hold office as, a member of the Board if the person is a disqualified person
A person may not be appointed to, or hold office as, a member of the Board if the person is not ordinarily resident in the Republic
A person may not be appointed to, or hold office as, a member of the Board if the person is engaged in the business of a financial institution
A person may not be appointed to, or hold office as, a member of the Board if the person is engaged in the provision of financial products or financial services to financial customers

sections.source_quote: 180. (1) The members of the Board are appointed by the Minister. (2) (a) The Minister must appoint a member as Chairperson and another member as Deputy Chairperson. (b) The Commissioner and the Chief Ombud may not be appointed as Chairperson or Deputy Chairperson.

sections.heading: (1) Tona o tshwanetse, go ya ka dikgato tse di maleba, go tlosa leloko la Boto mo
sections.title: Board member conduct and reporting
sections.summary: Board members must report to the Minister on compliance by ombud schemes with financial sector laws, trends in complaints, and conduct of financial institutions causing complaints. They must also address other matters assigned by financial sector laws. These requirements apply to the Board of the Ombud Council.

requirements.text: Keep the Minister informed of compliance by ombud schemes with financial sector laws in so far as they relate to ombud schemes
Keep the Minister informed of trends in the nature of complaints and issues raised in complaints that ombud schemes are dealing with
Keep the Minister informed of how those types of issues and complaints are being dealt with
Keep the financial sector regulators informed of the conduct of financial institutions that is giving rise to complaints to ombud schemes
Address any other matter assigned in terms of a financial sector law to the Board

sections.source_quote: 184. The Board must— (d) keep the Minister informed of— (i) compliance by ombud schemes with the financial sector laws in so far as they relate to ombud schemes; (ii) trends in the nature of complaints and issues raised in complaints that ombud schemes are dealing with, and how those types of issues and complaints are being dealt with; and (iii) the conduct of financial institutions that is giving rise to complaints to ombud schemes; and (e) keep the financial sector regulators informed of the conduct of financial institutions that is giving rise to complaints to ombud schemes; and (f) address any other matter assigned in terms of a financial sector law to the Board.

sections.heading: The Board must—
sections.title: Board responsibilities and oversight
sections.summary: The Board must oversee the Ombud Council's management and administration to ensure efficiency and effectiveness. It must appoint committee members, determine fees in accordance with financial sector laws, and monitor compliance with financial sector laws by ombud schemes. The Board also reports on complaint trends and financial institution conduct to the Minister and financial sector regulators.

requirements.text: Generally oversee the management and administration of the Ombud Council in order to ensure that it is efficient and effective
Appoint members of committees of the Ombud Council required or permitted by a law, and give directions regarding the conduct of the work of any committee
Make determinations of fees in terms of a financial sector law
Keep the Minister informed of compliance by ombud schemes with the financial sector laws in so far as they relate to ombud schemes
Keep the Minister informed of trends in the nature of complaints and issues raised in complaints that ombud schemes are dealing with
Keep the Minister informed of how those types of issues and complaints are being dealt with
Keep the financial sector regulators informed of the conduct of financial institutions that is giving rise to complaints to ombud schemes
Address any other matter assigned in terms of a financial sector law to the Board

sections.source_quote: 184. The Board must— (a) generally oversee the management and administration of the Ombud Council in order to ensure that it is efficient and effective; (b) appoint members of committees of the Ombud Council required or permitted by a law, and give directions regarding the conduct of the work of any committee; (c) make determinations of fees in terms of a financial sector law; (d) keep the Minister informed of— (i) compliance by ombud schemes with the financial sector laws in so far as they relate to ombud schemes; (ii) trends in the nature of complaints and issues raised in complaints that ombud schemes are dealing with, and how those types of issues and complaints are being dealt with; and (iii) the conduct of financial institutions that is giving rise to complaints to ombud schemes; and (e) keep the financial sector regulators informed of the conduct of financial institutions that is giving rise to complaints to ombud schemes; and (f) address any other matter assigned in terms of a financial sector law to the Board.

sections.heading: (1) (a) Tshitsinyo e e fa pele ga kopano ya Boto e nna tshwetso ya Boto fa bontsi
sections.title: Board decision validity and independence
sections.summary: Board decisions remain valid even if there was a vacancy in the office of a member when the decision was taken or if a person who was not a member participated in the decision but did not vote. This ensures the continuity and legitimacy of Board decisions.

requirements.text: A decision of the Board is not invalid merely because there was a vacancy in the office of a member when the decision was taken
A decision of the Board is not invalid merely because a person who was not a member participated in the decision, but did not vote

sections.source_quote: 186. (3) A decision of the Board is not invalid merely because— (a) there was a vacancy in the office of a member when the decision was taken; or (b) a person who was not a member participated in the decision, but did not vote.

sections.title: Disclosure of interests and conflicts
sections.summary: Board members must disclose any interests that could influence their decisions, including financial relationships, conflicts of interest, and other relevant matters. This ensures transparency and impartiality in decision-making processes.

Motho yo e leng kgotsa yo e neng e le leloko la badirimmogo ba Khansele ya Omb: (c) thatafaletsa motho yo mongwe
