(1) Motho yo e leng kgotsa yo e neng e le leloko la badirimmogo ba Khansele ya Ombud o ka se dirise maemo ao kgotsa tshedimosetso nngwe le nngwe e a e boneng jaaka leloko la badirimmogo go— (a) una molemo ka boena kgotsa go dira motho yo mongwe a une molemo ka tsela e e sa siamang; (b) kgoreletsa Khansele ya Ombud go dira ditiro tsa yona; kgotsa (c) thatafaletsa motho yo mongwe.

sections.heading: (1) A member of the Board must disclose, at a meeting of the Board, or in writing
sections.title: Board members must disclose conflicts of interest before decision-making
sections.summary: Board members must disclose any interest in a matter being considered by them, including related party interests, to other board members. Disclosure must occur as soon as practicable after becoming aware of the interest. Board members may not perform functions related to the matter unless they have disclosed the interest and other board members have determined the interest does not affect their proper execution of duties. Similar requirements apply to committees of the Ombud Council. The disclosure process includes both immediate and future interests, with exemptions for non-material interests and official roles.
sections.dtia_summary: Board members must disclose conflicts of interest before participating in decisions, ensuring transparency in financial governance. This affects DTIA by requiring clear conflict management protocols for cross-border or sensitive financial decisions.

requirements.text: Board members must disclose any interest in a matter being considered by them to other board members
Disclosure must occur as soon as practicable after becoming aware of the interest
Board members may not perform functions related to the matter unless they have disclosed the interest
Other board members must determine the interest does not affect the member's proper execution of duties
Similar requirements apply to committees of the Ombud Council
Exemptions apply for non-material interests and official roles

sections.source_quote: 193. (1) A member of the Board must disclose, at a meeting of the Board, or in writing to each of the other members, any interest in a matter that is being or will be considered by him or her, whether or not at a meeting of the Board, being an interest that— (a) the member has; or (b) a person who is a related party to the member has.

sections.heading: (1) The Ombud Council must determine an application for recognition in terms
sections.title: Ombud Council must approve industry ombud schemes
sections.summary: The Ombud Council must determine applications for recognition of industry ombud schemes. Recognition requires that a significant number of financial institutions join the scheme, governing rules identify financial products/services, require customer notifications, ensure complaint resolution, enforceable rules, adequate resources, and alignment with public interest. The Council must decide within three months, with conditions that may be imposed. Recognition is conditional on compliance with financial sector laws.
sections.dtia_summary: Ombud Council must approve industry ombud schemes that meet specific criteria for financial customer protection, affecting DTIA by requiring robust oversight mechanisms for cross-border dispute resolution.

requirements.text: Ombud Council must determine applications for recognition within three months
Governing rules must identify financial products/services the scheme relates to
Governing rules must require members to inform customers about the scheme
Governing rules must ensure adequate complaint resolution mechanisms
Governing rules must be legally binding and enforceable
Governing rules must require compliance with ombud determinations
Governing rules must provide adequate oversight of scheme operations
Governing rules must ensure equitable complaint handling
Scheme must have sufficient resources to comply with financial laws
Recognition must not be contrary to financial customers' interests

sections.source_quote: 196. (1) The Ombud Council must determine an application for recognition in terms of section 194 by— (a) granting the application and notifying the applicant accordingly; or (b) refusing the application and notifying the applicant accordingly.

sections.heading: (1) The Ombud Council may, by notice to a recognised industry ombud scheme,
sections.title: Ombud Council may suspend industry ombud schemes
sections.summary: The Ombud Council may suspend recognition of an industry ombud scheme if the scheme applies for suspension, contravenes recognition conditions, violates governing rules, provides false information, fails to comply with the Act, or if suspension is necessary to prevent serious contraventions of financial laws or material prejudice to customers. Suspension takes effect immediately upon notice and does not affect existing obligations under financial laws.
sections.dtia_summary: Ombud Council can suspend industry ombud schemes that fail to meet compliance standards, impacting DTIA by introducing risk-based suspension mechanisms for cross-border dispute resolution.

requirements.text: Ombud Council may suspend recognition if the scheme applies for suspension
Ombud Council may suspend recognition if conditions of recognition are contravened
Ombud Council may suspend recognition if governing rules are violated
Ombud Council may suspend recognition if false information is provided
Ombud Council may suspend recognition if the scheme fails to comply with the Act
Ombud Council may suspend recognition to prevent serious contraventions of financial laws
Ombud Council may suspend recognition to prevent material prejudice to customers
Ombud Council may suspend recognition if fees are unpaid for 30+ days

sections.source_quote: 198. (1) The Ombud Council may, by notice to a recognised industry ombud scheme, suspend the recognition of the scheme if— (a) the industry ombud scheme applies for suspension; (b) a condition of recognition has been contravened or not been complied with in a material way; (c) the industry ombud scheme, an ombud for the industry ombud scheme, or a significant number of the financial institutions that are members of the industry ombud scheme, have contravened in a material way the governing rules of the industry ombud scheme, a provision of a financial sector law relating to ombuds or Ombud Council rules;

sections.heading: (1) (a) Pele Khansele ya Ombud e fetola lebaka la, kgotsa e sekega kgotsa e
sections.title: Ombud Council must ensure ombud compliance
sections.summary: The Ombud Council must ensure ombuds comply with their obligations, including timely disclosure of interests that could affect their functions. The Council must maintain a register of all disclosures and decisions made under this section. Ombuds must make timely, proper, and adequate disclosures of interests, including related party interests, that could affect their proper execution of functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Ombud Council requires ombuds to disclose conflicts of interest, impacting DTIA by ensuring transparency in dispute resolution processes for financial customers.

requirements.text: Ombuds must make timely, proper, and adequate disclosure of interests
Ombuds must disclose related party interests
Ombud Council must maintain a register of all disclosures and decisions

sections.source_quote: 200. (1) The Ombud Council must ensure ombuds comply with their obligations, including timely disclosure of interests that could affect their functions.

sections.heading: (1) The Ombud Council may make rules for, or in respect of, ombuds and ombud
sections.title: Ombud Council rules must ensure independence and compliance
sections.summary: Ombud Council rules must not conflict with financial sector laws or interfere with ombud independence. Rules must promote consistent approaches, efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and coordination between ombud schemes. Rules must consider differences in complaint complexity across schemes.
sections.dtia_summary: Ombud Council rules must ensure ombud independence and compliance, affecting DTIA by standardizing dispute resolution mechanisms for financial customers.

requirements.text: Ombud Council rules must not conflict with financial sector laws
Ombud Council rules must not interfere with ombud independence
Rules must promote consistent approaches across ombud schemes
Rules must promote efficiency and cost-effectiveness of ombud schemes
Rules must promote coordination and cooperation between ombud schemes
Rules must consider differences in complaint complexity

sections.source_quote: 201. (3) An Ombud Council rule must not be inconsistent with relevant financial sector laws.

sections.heading: (1) The Ombud Council may issue to a person who is an ombud, or to an ombud
sections.title: Ombud Council may issue directives to ombuds
sections.summary: The Ombud Council may issue written directives to ombuds or ombud schemes requiring action if they have contravened financial laws. Directives must aim to achieve the Council's objectives and stop contraventions. The Council must provide draft directives and reasons for issuance, invite submissions, and consider submissions before issuing directives. Directives must specify reasonable compliance periods.
sections.dtia_summary: Ombud Council can issue directives to ombuds for compliance with financial laws, impacting DTIA by enabling corrective actions for cross-border dispute resolution.

requirements.text: Ombud Council may issue written directives to ombuds or schemes
Directives must aim to achieve the Council's objectives
Directives must stop contraventions of financial laws
Ombud Council must provide draft directives and reasons for issuance
Ombud Council must invite submissions on the directive
Ombud Council must consider all submissions before issuing directives
Directives must specify reasonable compliance periods

sections.source_quote: 202. (1) The Ombud Council may issue to a person who is an ombud, or to an ombud scheme, a written directive requiring the person to take action specified in the directive if the person has contravened or is likely to contravene a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes.

sections.heading: (1) An ombud scheme may give the Ombud Council, and the Ombud Council
sections.title: Ombud schemes' written undertakings for financial sector compliance
sections.summary: An ombud scheme can provide a written undertaking to the Ombud Council regarding its future conduct under financial sector laws specific to ombud schemes. Section 151 applies to such undertakings with references to 'responsible authority' interpreted as the Ombud Council.

requirements.text: An ombud scheme may give the Ombud Council a written undertaking concerning its future conduct in relation to a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes

sections.source_quote: 203. (1) An ombud scheme may give the Ombud Council, and the Ombud Council may accept, a written undertaking concerning the ombud scheme's future conduct in relation to a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes.

sections.heading: (1) The Ombud Council may commence proceedings against an ombud scheme
sections.title: Ombud Council's authority to commence proceedings for compliance
sections.summary: The Ombud Council can initiate legal proceedings in the High Court to enforce compliance with financial sector laws specific to ombud schemes. Section 152 applies to these proceedings with 'responsible authority' interpreted as the Ombud Council.

requirements.text: The Ombud Council may commence proceedings against an ombud scheme in the High Court for an order to ensure compliance with the financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes

sections.source_quote: 204. (1) The Ombud Council may commence proceedings against an ombud scheme in the High Court for an order to ensure compliance with the financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes.

sections.heading: (1) The Ombud Council may make a debarment order in respect of a natural
sections.title: Debarment orders for ombud scheme personnel
sections.summary: The Ombud Council can issue debarment orders prohibiting individuals from performing roles in ombud schemes for specified periods if they have contravened financial sector laws related to ombud schemes or Ombud Council rules. The process requires providing draft orders, inviting submissions, and considering regulator advice.

requirements.text: The Ombud Council may make a debarment order in respect of a natural person if the person has contravened a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes or an Ombud Council rule

The Ombud Council may make a debarment order in respect of a natural person if the person has attempted, or conspired with, aided, abetted, induced, incited or procured another person to contravene a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes
Before making a debarment order, the Ombud Council must give a draft of the order to the person and to financial sector regulators with reasons and relevant information
Before making a debarment order, the Ombud Council must invite the person to make submissions and give them a reasonable period to do so
The period for submissions must be at least one month
The Ombud Council must take into account any submission made by or for the person
The Ombud Council must take into account any advice from a financial sector regulator
A debarment order prohibits the person from performing a specified role in relation to an ombud scheme for a specified period
A person subject to a debarment order may not engage in conduct that directly or indirectly contravenes the order
A person contravenes the debarment order if they enter into an arrangement with another person to engage in conduct for or on behalf of the person
An ombud scheme that becomes aware of a debarment order must take all reasonable steps to ensure the order is given effect to

sections.source_quote: 205. (1) The Ombud Council may make a debarment order in respect of a natural person if the person has— (a) contravened a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes, or an Ombud Council rule; (b) attempted, or conspired with, aided, abetted, induced, incited or procured another person to contravene a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes.

sections.heading: (1) Chapter 13 applies in relation to the Ombud Council as if references in that
sections.title: Ombud Council's application of Chapter 13
sections.summary: Chapter 13 applies to the Ombud Council with references to financial sector laws interpreted as those specific to ombud schemes and financial sector regulators interpreted as the Ombud Council. The Ombud Council may impose administrative penalties on ombud schemes, their governing bodies, or ombuds.

requirements.text: Chapter 13 applies in relation to the Ombud Council as if references to a financial sector law were references to a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes
Chapter 13 applies in relation to the Ombud Council as if references to a financial sector regulator were references to the Ombud Council
The Ombud Council may impose an administrative penalty only on an ombud scheme, a member of the governing body of an ombud scheme, or an ombud

sections.source_quote: 206. (1) Chapter 13 applies in relation to the Ombud Council as if references in that Chapter— (a) to a financial sector law were references to a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes; and (b) to a financial sector regulator were references to the Ombud Council.

sections.heading: (1) (a) The Ombud Council may, by written notice, require an ombud scheme or
sections.title: Ombud Council's information requests for compliance
sections.summary: The Ombud Council can require ombud schemes or ombuds to provide specified information or documents relevant to compliance with financial sector laws specific to ombuds, Ombud Council rules, directives, or enforceable undertakings. The information must be verified by an auditor approved by the Ombud Council.

requirements.text: The Ombud Council may require an ombud scheme or an ombud to provide specified information or a specified document relevant to compliance with financial sector laws in so far as they relate to ombuds
The Ombud Council may require the information or document to be verified as specified in the notice, including by an auditor approved by the Ombud Council

sections.source_quote: 207. (1) (a) The Ombud Council may, by written notice, require an ombud scheme or an ombud to provide specified information or a specified document in the possession or under the control of the person to whom the notice is given, being information or a document which is relevant to the Ombud Council's assessment of compliance by an ombud scheme or an ombud with— (i) a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombuds; (ii) an Ombud Council rule; (iii) a directive issued by the Ombud Council in terms of section 202; or (iv) an enforceable undertaking accepted by the Ombud Council.

sections.heading: (1) Part 3 of Chapter 9 applies in relation to the Ombud Council as if—
sections.title: Ombud Council's supervisory inspections
sections.summary: Part 3 of Chapter 9 applies to the Ombud Council with references to financial sector laws interpreted as those specific to ombud schemes and financial sector regulators interpreted as the Ombud Council. The purpose of on-site inspections is to check compliance with financial sector laws specific to ombuds.

requirements.text: Part 3 of Chapter 9 applies in relation to the Ombud Council as if references to a financial sector law were references to a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes
Part 3 of Chapter 9 applies in relation to the Ombud Council as if references to a financial sector regulator were references to the Ombud Council
Part 3 of Chapter 9 applies in relation to the Ombud Council as if references to a supervised entity were references to an ombud scheme or an ombud
The purpose of a supervisory on-site inspection of an ombud scheme or ombud is to check compliance with financial sector laws in so far as they relate to ombuds
Part 4 of Chapter 9 applies in relation to the Ombud Council as if references to a financial sector law were references to a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes
Part 4 of Chapter 9 applies in relation to the Ombud Council as if section 135(1)(b) were omitted
Part 4 of Chapter 9 applies in relation to the Ombud Council as if references to a financial sector regulator were references to the Ombud Council
Section 140 applies in relation to the Ombud Council exercising powers in terms of this section as it applies in relation to the financial sector regulators

sections.source_quote: 208. (1) Part 3 of Chapter 9 applies in relation to the Ombud Council as if— (a) references in that Chapter to a financial sector law were references to a financial sector law in so far as it relates to ombud schemes; (b) references to a financial sector regulator were references to the Ombud Council; and (c) references to a supervised entity were references to an ombud scheme or an ombud.

sections.heading: (1) The Ombud Council must, as soon as practicable after this Part comes into
sections.title: Establishment of financial customer complaint resolution centres
sections.summary: The Ombud Council must establish complaint resolution centres for financial customers within 30 days of this Part coming into effect. These centres provide staff and facilities to help customers formulate complaints and identify appropriate ombuds for resolution. Financial institutions may incorporate call centres into these centres to facilitate customer access.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to provide accessible complaint resolution mechanisms for customers, which may impact data transfer if customer complaints involve cross-border financial data.

requirements.text: The Ombud Council must establish and operate one or more centres to facilitate financial customers' access to appropriate ombuds
A centre may incorporate a call centre
Centres must provide staff and facilities to assist financial customers in formulating complaints and identifying appropriate ombuds

sections.source_quote: 209. (1) The Ombud Council must, as soon as practicable after this Part comes into effect, establish and operate one or more centres to facilitate financial customers' access to appropriate ombuds.

sections.heading: (1) A financial institution may not describe any internal procedure it has for
sections.title: Prohibition on using internal complaint procedures as ombud schemes
sections.summary: Financial institutions cannot describe internal procedures for resolving customer complaints as ombud schemes. They must disclose applicable ombud schemes and how to contact them to customers. Financial institutions may not require customers to submit complaints to ombuds unless the ombud is part of a recognised industry scheme or statutory scheme. Such requirements are void if contrary to this provision.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts financial institutions from using internal procedures as ombud schemes, ensuring complaints are handled through external, recognised mechanisms which may affect data transfer pathways.

requirements.text: A financial institution may not describe any internal procedure for dealing with or resolving complaints as an ombud scheme
A financial institution must disclose applicable ombud schemes and how to contact them to customers
A financial institution may not require customers to submit complaints to an ombud unless the ombud is part of a recognised industry scheme or statutory scheme
Requirements contrary to the above are void

sections.source_quote: 210. (1) A financial institution may not describe any internal procedure it has for dealing with or resolving complaints made to it by financial customers as an ombud scheme, or a person that deals with or resolves such complaints as an ombud.

sections.heading: The ombud schemes, and the ombuds, must cooperate and collaborate with each
sections.title: Cooperation between ombud schemes and ombuds
sections.summary: Ombud schemes and ombuds must cooperate and collaborate on complaints about financial institutions regarding financial products and services. This includes developing joint processes to hear and determine complaints, either on their own initiative or as required by Ombud Council rules.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires coordination between ombud schemes for complaint resolution, which may impact data sharing and cross-border complaint handling mechanisms.

requirements.text: Ombud schemes and ombuds must cooperate and collaborate with each other regarding complaints about financial institutions
They must develop processes and procedures to jointly hear and determine complaints

sections.source_quote: 213. The ombud schemes, and the ombuds, must cooperate and collaborate with each other regarding complaints about financial institutions in relation to financial products and financial services, including by developing processes and procedures to jointly hear and determine complaints, on their own initiative or as may be required by Ombud Council rules.

sections.heading: (1) Before the Ombud Council can recognise an industry ombud scheme in terms
sections.title: Ombud scheme recognition process
sections.summary: Before recognising an industry ombud scheme, the Ombud Council must publish draft governing rules, explain their purpose, state expected impact, and invite submissions. The submission period must be at least 30 days. Governing rules require Ombud Council approval and cannot be amended without it. Rules adopted without approval are void.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets the process for ombud scheme recognition, which affects the legal framework for data transfer and complaint resolution across borders.

requirements.text: Ombud Council must publish draft governing rules or amendments to governing rules
Ombud Council must publish a statement explaining the need for and intended operation of governing rules
Ombud Council must publish a statement of expected impact of governing rules
Ombud Council must publish a notice inviting submissions on governing rules
Submission period for governing rules must be at least 30 days
Governing rules must be approved by Ombud Council
Governing rules cannot be amended without Ombud Council approval
Governing rules adopted without Ombud Council approval are void

sections.source_quote: 214. (1) Before the Ombud Council can recognise an industry ombud scheme in terms of section 194, the Ombud Council must— (a) publish— (i) a draft of the governing rules or amendments to the governing rules; (ii) a statement explaining the need for and the intended operation of the governing rules or the amendment to the governing rules; (iii) a statement of the expected impact of the governing rules or the amendment to the governing rules; and (iv) a notice inviting submissions in relation to the rules or amendment to the governing rules and stating where, how and by when submissions are to be made; and (b) submit the draft governing rules to the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.

sections.heading: (1) A financial institution that is a member of a recognised industry ombud
sections.title: Compliance with ombud scheme governing rules
sections.summary: Financial institutions that are members of recognised industry ombud schemes must comply with the governing rules of the scheme. Financial customers can enforce this obligation as if it were a contractual provision for financial products or services.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to follow ombud scheme rules, which may impact data transfer and processing if the scheme involves cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: Financial institutions that are members of recognised industry ombud schemes must comply with the governing rules of the scheme
Financial customers may enforce compliance with governing rules as if it were a contractual provision

sections.source_quote: 215. (1) A financial institution that is a member of a recognised industry ombud scheme must comply with the governing rules of the scheme.

sections.heading: Receipt of a complaint by a financial sector regulator, the Ombud Council or an
sections.title: Suspension of time barring terms for complaints
sections.summary: Receipt of a complaint by a financial sector regulator, Ombud Council, or ombud suspends any applicable time barring terms, including prescription periods under the Prescription Act, 1969, from the date of complaint receipt until the complaint is withdrawn or resolved.
sections.dtia_summary: Suspends time bars for complaints, which may affect data retention and processing timelines for cross-border complaints.

requirements.text: Receipt of a complaint by a financial sector regulator, Ombud Council, or ombud suspends time barring terms
Suspension applies from receipt of complaint until complaint is withdrawn or finally determined

sections.source_quote: 216. Receipt of a complaint by a financial sector regulator, the Ombud Council or an ombud suspends any applicable time barring terms, whether in terms of an agreement or any law, or the running of prescription in terms of the Prescription Act, 1969 (), for the period from the receipt of the complaint until the complaint has either been withdrawn or finally determined.

sections.heading: (1) An ombud scheme must—
sections.title: Ombud scheme annual reporting and information submission requirements
sections.summary: An ombud scheme must submit an annual report to the Ombud Council detailing compliance with financial sector laws, complaints handled, and conduct causing complaints. It must also comply with any information requests from the Ombud Council. Financial institutions must provide information to the Financial Sector Conduct Authority upon request regarding ombud schemes and financial institution conduct trends. If an ombud becomes aware of material contraventions by financial institutions or actions affecting customers, it must report to the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires ombud schemes to report on financial institution conduct and complaints, which impacts data transfer assessments by mandating transparency in financial institution behavior monitoring.

requirements.text: Submit annual report to Ombud Council within six months of financial year end
Report on compliance with financial sector laws related to ombud schemes
Report on complaints handled and how they are dealt with
Report on financial institution conduct causing complaints
Comply with Ombud Council's information requests about ombud schemes
Provide information to Financial Sector Conduct Authority upon request
Report material contraventions by financial institutions to Financial Sector Conduct Authority
Report actions by financial institutions affecting customers to Financial Sector Conduct Authority

sections.source_quote: 217. (1) An ombud scheme must— (a) within six months after the end of each financial year, submit to the Ombud Council, in the form and with the content required by the Ombud Council, a report on the operation of the ombud scheme during the financial year, including in relation to— (i) compliance with the financial sector laws in so far as they relate to ombud schemes; (ii) the complaints that the ombud scheme is dealing with, and how they are being dealt with; and (iii) the conduct of financial institutions that is giving rise to complaints; (b) comply with any request by the Ombud Council at any time for information about the operation of the ombud scheme, trends in and implications of the conduct of financial institutions observed by the ombud scheme, and any other relevant information.

sections.heading: For the purposes of this Chapter—
sections.title: Definitions of key terms for financial decisions and decision-makers
sections.summary: This section defines 'decision' and 'decision-maker' in the context of financial services. A 'decision' includes regulatory decisions, ombud decisions, market infrastructure decisions, and specific actions or omissions. A 'decision-maker' refers to entities responsible for making decisions, such as financial sector regulators, the Ombud Council, authorized providers, market infrastructure, and statutory ombuds.

requirements.text: Decision includes regulatory decisions by financial sector regulators
Decision includes ombud decisions in financial sector laws
Decision includes market infrastructure decisions under Financial Markets Act
Decision includes statutory ombud decisions on specific complaints
Decision includes omissions to take decisions within prescribed periods
Decision includes omissions to take decisions within reasonable periods
Decision includes actions taken as a result of decisions
Decision includes omissions to take action as a result of decisions
Decision does not include regulatory decisions directed to take actions
Decision does not include supervisory inspections or investigations
Decision includes assessments of levies issued to specific persons
Decision-maker refers to financial sector regulators for regulatory decisions
Decision-maker refers to Ombud Council for ombud decisions
Decision-maker refers to authorized financial services providers for decisions under Financial Advisory Act
Decision-maker refers to market infrastructure for decisions under Financial Markets Act
Decision-maker refers to statutory ombuds for ombud decisions

sections.source_quote: 218. For the purposes of this Chapter— 'decision' means each of the following: (a) A decision by a financial sector regulator or the Ombud Council in terms of a financial sector law in relation to a specific person; (b) a decision by an authorised financial services provider, as defined in section 1 of the Financial Advisory and Intermediary Services Act, in terms of section 14 of that Act in relation to a specific person; (c) a decision in relation to a specific person by a market infrastructure, being a decision in terms of rules of the market infrastructure contemplated by the Financial Markets Act, or a decision contemplated in section 105 of the Financial Markets Act; (d) a decision of a statutory ombud in terms of a financial sector law in relation to a specific complaint by a person; (e) a decision of a kind prescribed by Regulation for the purposes of this paragraph, and includes— (f) an omission to take such a decision within the period prescribed or specified in a financial sector law, rules, or other requirements pertaining to the decision-maker; (g) an omission to take such a decision within a reasonable period, if the applicable financial sector law, or rules of, or other requirements pertaining to, the decision-maker require the decision to be taken but without prescribing or specifying a period; (h) an action taken as a result of such a decision; and (i) an omission to take action as a result of such a decision within the prescribed or a reasonable period, if the applicable financial sector law requires the action to be taken but does not prescribe a period, but does not include— (j) a decision of a financial sector regulator that the financial sector regulator is directed to take in terms of section 18(2) or 30(1); (k) a decision to conduct a supervisory on-site inspection or an investigation; (l) an assessment of a levy issued to a specific person; or (m) a decision prescribed by Regulations made for this paragraph; 'decision-maker' means— (a) in relation to a decision by a financial sector regulator, the financial sector regulator; (b) in relation to a decision by the Ombud Council, the Ombud Council; (c) in relation to a decision referred to in paragraph (b) of the definition of 'decision' in this section, the authorised financial services provider; (d) in relation to a decision referred to in paragraph (c) of the definition of 'decision' in this section, the market infrastructure; (e) in relation to a decision by a statutory ombud, the statutory ombud; and (f) in relation to a decision referred to in paragraph (e) of the definition of 'decision' in this section, the person identified in the Regulations as the decision-maker.

sections.heading: (1) The Financial Services Tribunal is hereby established to reconsider, in terms
sections.title: Establishment of Financial Services Tribunal for reconsidering decisions
sections.summary: The Financial Services Tribunal is established to reconsider decisions defined in section 218 and perform other functions as conferred by the Act and specific financial sector laws. The Tribunal must be independent, impartial, a tribunal of record, and operate in accordance with the Act and financial sector laws.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes a tribunal to review financial decisions, which impacts data transfer assessments by providing a mechanism for resolving disputes over financial data handling.

requirements.text: Financial Services Tribunal is established to reconsider decisions defined in section 218
Tribunal must be independent
Tribunal must be impartial and exercise powers without fear, favour or prejudice
Tribunal must be a tribunal of record
Tribunal must perform functions in accordance with the Act and specific financial sector laws

sections.source_quote: 219. (1) The Financial Services Tribunal is hereby established to reconsider, in terms of this Chapter, decisions as defined in section 218 and to perform the other functions conferred on it by this Act and specific financial sector laws. (2) The Tribunal— (a) is independent; (b) must be impartial and exercise its powers without fear, favour or prejudice; (c) is a tribunal of record; and (d) must perform its function in accordance with this Act and the specific financial sector laws.

sections.heading: (1) Tona o tshwanetse go tlhoma le go tlamela lenane la batho ba ba
sections.title: Panel list management for financial services regulators
sections.summary: Regulators must manage a panel list of individuals who can act as panel members. The panel list must be updated based on specific criteria, including requests from individuals, disqualifications, health issues, failure to discharge responsibilities, and inconsistent conduct. Regulators must disclose interests of panel members.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulates panel list management for financial services regulators, which impacts data transfer assessments by ensuring transparency in panel member qualifications and disclosures.

requirements.text: Regulators must remove a person from the panel list if they request it
Regulators must remove a person from the panel list if they become disqualified
Regulators may remove a person from the panel list if they are unable to act due to health or other reasons
Regulators may remove a person from the panel list if they have materially failed in responsibilities
Regulators may remove a person from the panel list if they have acted inconsistently with panel member duties
Regulators must disclose interests of panel members

sections.source_quote: 225. (1) Tona o tshwanetse go tlhoma le go tlamela lenane la batho ba ba ikemiseditseng go dira jaaka maloko a dipanele tsa Lekgotla. (2) Batho ba ba akareditsweng mo lenaneng la panele ba tshwanetse— (a) go nna le maitemogelo mo kgotsa kitso ya boitseanape— (i) ya molao; kgotsa (ii) ya dikuno tsa ditsˇhelete,ditirelo tsa ditsˇhelete, didiriso tsa ditsˇhelete, dithulaganyetso tsa popegotheo ya mmaraka kgotsa dithulaganyo tsa ditsˇhelete; le (b) go nna motho yo o itekanetseng e bile a le matshwanedi go ka akaretswa mo lenaneng la panele. (3) Motho o ka se akaretswe mo lenaneng la panele fa e le motho yo o ileditsweng. (4) Tona o ka, dingwaga dingwe le dingwe tse tlhano, laletsa mo phatlalatseng batho go dira kopo ya go akaretswa mo lenaneng la panele. (5) Modulasetilo o tshwanetse go netefatsa gore batho ba ba akareditsweng mo lenaneng la panele ba newa tsˇhono e e lekanang ya go thapiwa go dira mo paneleng ya Lekgotla. (6) The Minister— (a) must remove a person from the panel list— (i) if the person so requests; or (ii) if the person becomes a disqualified person; and (b) may, on recommendation of the Chairperson, remove a person from the panel list if the person— (i) is unable to act as a panel member for health or other reasons; (ii) has failed in a material way to discharge any of the responsibilities of a panel member; or (iii) has acted in a way that is inconsistent with acting as a panel member.

sections.heading: An obligation in a financial sector law to notify a person of a decision taken in
sections.title: Right to request reasons for decisions and reconsideration
sections.summary: Financial sector laws that require notification of decisions must also include the right to request reasons for decisions as defined in section 229 and the right to have decisions reconsidered as per Part 4 of the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures individuals have rights to request reasons and reconsider decisions, which impacts data transfer assessments by requiring transparency in decision-making processes.

requirements.text: Obligation to notify person of right to request reasons for decisions
Obligation to notify person of right to have decisions reconsidered

sections.source_quote: 228. An obligation in a financial sector law to notify a person of a decision taken in relation to that person must be read as including an obligation to notify the person of that person's right— (a) to request reasons for the decision in terms of section 229; and (b) to have the decision reconsidered in terms of Part 4.

sections.heading: (1) A fee or levy payable to a financial sector body in terms of section 237 is a
sections.title: Fee or levy as debt payable to financial sector body
sections.summary: This section defines a fee or levy payable to a financial sector body as a debt due to that body. It permits the financial sector body to recover this debt through judicial processes in a competent court. The debt is established as a legal obligation that can be enforced via court proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines fees and levies as debts that can be recovered through judicial processes, relevant for DTIA as it establishes a legal mechanism for debt recovery across borders.

requirements.text: A fee or levy payable to a financial sector body in terms of section 237 is a debt due to the financial sector body
A financial sector body may recover the amount of a debt due in terms of this section by way of a judicial process in a competent court

sections.source_quote: 238. (1) A fee or levy payable to a financial sector body in terms of section 237 is a debt due to the financial sector body. (2) A financial sector body may recover the amount of a debt due in terms of this section by way of a judicial process in a competent court.

sections.heading: (1) For each financial year, each financial sector body must prepare and adopt—
sections.title: Financial sector bodies must prepare annual budget and fee proposals
sections.summary: Each financial sector body must prepare and adopt an annual budget including expenditure estimates, fee and levy proposals, and projected expenditure for the next two financial years. The budget must account for special levies and reserves, with total reserves not exceeding 15% of total expenditure excluding reserves. The body must consider submissions received on the budget and fee proposals.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial sector bodies to prepare detailed financial plans that impact fee and levy structures, relevant for DTIA as it affects financial data handling across borders.

requirements.text: For each financial year, each financial sector body must prepare and adopt a budget in accordance with section 248 that includes an estimate of its expenditure
For each financial year, each financial sector body must prepare and adopt a proposal for the fees that will be charged and levies that will be imposed by the financial sector body
For each financial year, each financial sector body must prepare and adopt projected estimates of its expenditure for next 2 financial years

A proposal for levies may include a proposal for one or more special levies, and in that case, the estimate of expenditure must include an estimate for the special expenditure in relation to a special levy proposal

An estimate of expenditure for a financial year may include provision for one or more reserves, but the total accumulated reserves included in the estimate of expenditure may not exceed 15% of the total estimated expenditure, excluding the reserves
The financial sector body must take into account submissions made in respect of the budget as well as the fees and levies proposals, which it receives in terms of section

sections.source_quote: 239. (1) For each financial year, each financial sector body must prepare and adopt— (a) a budget in accordance with section 248 that includes an estimate of its expenditure; (b) a proposal for the fees that will be charged and levies that will be imposed by the financial sector body; and (c) projected estimates of its expenditure for next 2 financial years.

sections.heading: (1) A financial sector body must issue to each supervised entity that is liable to
sections.title: Financial sector body must issue levy assessment notices
sections.summary: A financial sector body must issue to each supervised entity liable to pay a levy an assessment notice specifying the levy amount and due date. The notice must state the due date, which cannot be less than 30 days from when the entity receives the notice.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely levy assessments with clear due dates, relevant for DTIA as it impacts financial data transfer timelines and compliance.

requirements.text: A financial sector body must issue to each supervised entity that is liable to pay a levy for the financial year, an assessment of a levy payable by the supervised entity

The assessment notice issued to a supervised entity must state the date on which the levy is due and must be paid, which period must not be less than 30 days from the date of receipt of the notice of assessment by the supervised entity

sections.source_quote: 242. (1) A financial sector body must issue to each supervised entity that is liable to pay a levy for the financial year, an assessment of a levy payable by the supervised entity. (2) The assessment notice issued to a supervised entity must state the date on which the levy is due and must be paid, which period must not be less than 30 days from the date of receipt of the notice of assessment by the supervised entity.

sections.heading: (1) A person who has been charged a fee, or a supervised entity who has been
sections.title: Option to pay fees/levies by instalments
sections.summary: A person or supervised entity charged a fee or levy may offer to pay by instalments. If an offer is made, the financial sector body must accept, accept a modified offer, or reject the offer and notify the person accordingly. Offers must be made within 14 days of notification if the fee must be paid within 14 days, or at least 14 days before the due date otherwise. The body must notify the person within seven days of receiving the offer.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a mechanism for instalment payments, relevant for DTIA as it affects payment timelines and financial data handling across borders.

requirements.text: A person who has been charged a fee, or a supervised entity who has been charged a levy, may offer to pay the fee or levy by specified instalments
If an offer is made, the financial sector body must accept the offer; accept a modified offer; or reject the offer, and must notify the person who made the offer accordingly

A person who wishes to make an offer to pay a fee or levy by instalments must make an offer immediately after being notified of the fee or levy charged, if the fee or levy must be paid within 14 days after the date on which notification is received
A person who wishes to make an offer to pay a fee or levy by instalments must make an offer at least 14 days before the date on which the fee or levy must be paid, if paragraph (a) does not apply

The financial sector body must notify the person who made an offer in terms of subsection (1) of its decision immediately after receipt of the offer, in respect of an offer referred to in subsection (2)(a)

The financial sector body must notify the person who made an offer in terms of subsection (1) of its decision within seven days after the receipt of the offer, in respect of an offer referred to in subsection (2)(b)

sections.source_quote: 243. (1) A person who has been charged a fee, or a supervised entity who has been charged a levy, may offer to pay the fee or levy by specified instalments, and if an offer is made, the financial sector body must— (a) accept the offer; (b) accept a modified offer; or (c) reject the offer, and must notify the person who made the offer accordingly.

sections.heading: (1) If a fee or levy is not paid, or not paid in full, within the period specified for
sections.title: Interest on late or non-payment of fees and levies
sections.summary: If a fee or levy is not paid within the specified period and no instalment offer has been accepted, the person liable must pay interest at the rate prescribed in the Prescribed Rate of Interest Act, 1975. Interest is calculated on the unpaid amount and is a debt that can be recovered through judicial processes.
sections.dtia_summary: Imposes interest on late payments, relevant for DTIA as it affects financial data handling and cross-border payment compliance.

requirements.text: If a fee or levy is not paid, or not paid in full, within the period specified for payment, and an offer to pay the fee or levy by instalments has not been accepted as referred to in section 243(1)(a) or (b), the person liable to pay the fee or levy in question must pay interest at the rate referred to in subsection (2), on the amount of the fee or levy that remains unpaid 30 days after the due date
Interest due and payable on an outstanding fee or levy amount must be calculated based on the interest rate prescribed for the time being in terms of the Prescribed Rate of Interest Act, 1975
Interest charged is a debt due to the financial sector body, and may be recovered by a judicial process in a competent court

sections.source_quote: 244. (1) If a fee or levy is not paid, or not paid in full, within the period specified for payment, and an offer to pay the fee or levy by instalments has not been accepted as referred to in section 243(1)(a) or (b), the person liable to pay the fee or levy in question must pay interest at the rate referred to in subsection (2), on the amount of the fee or levy that remains unpaid 30 days after the due date.

sections.heading: (1) A financial sector body may, on application by a person who is liable to pay
sections.title: Fee exemption applications by financial sector bodies
sections.summary: Financial sector bodies can exempt fee payers from fee payments or partial fees upon application, subject to conditions set by the body. Applications must include specified particulars. Exemptions are only granted for sound reasons. This applies to entities liable to pay fees to financial sector bodies.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines conditions for fee exemptions, which may impact financial data transfers if exemptions affect fee collection mechanisms across borders.

requirements.text: Financial sector body may exempt person liable to pay fee from fee payment or part of fee
Application must include particulars determined by financial sector body
Exemption granted only for sound reasons

sections.source_quote: 245. (1) A financial sector body may, on application by a person who is liable to pay a fee, exempt the person from the payment of a fee, or a part of a fee, to the extent and subject to conditions determined by the financial sector body.

sections.heading: (1) Fees determined in accordance with section 237(1)(a), and interest accrued
sections.title: Designated bank accounts for fee and levy collections
sections.summary: Financial sector bodies must collect fees and levies into designated bank accounts under the control of the body. Levies must be paid into accounts controlled by the Financial Sector Conduct Authority. Designated accounts require approval from the National Treasury. Payment schedules must be agreed between the body and the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies bank account requirements for fee and levy collections, which could affect data transfer mechanisms if financial data is held in designated accounts across borders.

requirements.text: Fees and interest accrued must be collected by financial sector body and paid into designated bank account
Levies and interest accrued must be collected by Financial Sector Conduct Authority and paid into designated bank account
Allocations of levies must be transferred to financial sector body's designated account per agreed payment schedule
Designated bank accounts must be approved by National Treasury

sections.source_quote: 246. (1) Fees determined in accordance with section 237(1)(a), and interest accrued on fees in terms of section 244 must be collected by the financial sector body and paid into a bank account designated for that purpose, which is in the name and control of the financial sector body.

sections.heading: (1) The money of each financial sector body consists of—
sections.title: Composition and application of financial sector body funds
sections.summary: Financial sector bodies' funds consist of fees, levies, and interest from accounts. These funds may only be used for administrative costs, exercising powers under financial sector laws, or repaying amounts paid in error. This section defines permissible fund usage for financial sector bodies.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details fund composition and usage, which may impact data transfer if financial data is processed using these funds across borders.

requirements.text: Money of financial sector body consists of fees and levies received, funds from other sources, and interest on credit balances
Money may be applied only for general administrative costs, exercising powers under financial sector laws, or repaying amounts paid in error

sections.source_quote: 247. (1) The money of each financial sector body consists of— (a) amounts received by the financial sector body as fees and levies; (b) funds accruing to the financial sector body from any other source; and (c) interest on amounts standing to the credit of the financial sector body in an account.

sections.heading: (1) The accounting authority of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority, the
sections.title: Accounting authority for financial sector bodies
sections.summary: Financial sector bodies must comply with Public Finance Management Act requirements for accounting. The Financial Sector Conduct Authority, Ombud Council, Pension Funds Adjudicator, and Ombud for Financial Services Providers have specific accounting duties. The Prudential Authority's Chief Executive Officer is responsible for accounting. All bodies must prepare budgets, financial statements, and reports as required by law.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates accounting compliance for financial sector bodies, which could affect data transfer if financial data is processed in accordance with these accounting requirements across borders.

sections.heading: The Chairperson of the Tribunal is responsible to ensure that the functions and
sections.title: Tribunal Chairperson ensures functions are performed
sections.summary: The Chairperson of the Tribunal is responsible for ensuring that all functions and duties of the Tribunal under this Chapter are carried out. This section establishes the Chairperson's accountability for the Tribunal's operational effectiveness within the financial sector regulation framework.

This section outlines the obligations for information sharing between financial regulators and designated authorities.

(1) (a) An auditor of a licensed financial institution, or of a holding company of
sections.title: Auditor reporting obligations to financial regulators
sections.summary: Auditors of licensed financial institutions or holding companies of financial conglomerates must promptly submit detailed written reports to the Prudential Authority and the institution's governing body about matters they become aware of during audits that could cause financial unsoundness, contravene financial sector laws, or result in incomplete audits. These reports also include specific documents required by the Auditing Profession Act, 2005. Auditors who resign or have appointments terminated must provide written statements on resignation reasons and any pending reports they would have submitted.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires auditors to report financial risks and potential law violations to regulators, which impacts DTIA by mandating disclosure of material risks before cross-border financial transfers or operations.

Auditor must submit detailed written report about matters contravening financial sector laws
Auditor must submit detailed written report about matters likely to result in incomplete audits

Resigning auditor must submit any reports they would have submitted

sections.heading: (1) A person may report to a financial sector regulator—
sections.title: Reporting to financial sector regulators without liability
sections.summary: Any person may report suspected financial difficulties, contraventions of financial sector laws, or involvement in financial crime to a financial sector regulator. Reporters are not criminally liable or liable for compensation for losses caused by the report, provided the report was not made in bad faith.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows third parties to report financial risks to regulators without legal consequences, which affects DTIA by enabling external monitoring of cross-border financial activities.

requirements.text: Person may report suspected financial difficulties in a financial institution
Person may report suspected contraventions of financial sector laws
Person may report suspected involvement in financial crime
Person not criminally liable for making report unless in bad faith
Person not liable for compensation for losses caused by report unless in bad faith

sections.source_quote: 253. (1) A person may report to a financial sector regulator— (a) financial difficulties or suspected financial difficulties in a financial institution; (b) a contravention or suspected contravention of a financial sector law in relation to a financial institution; or (c) the involvement or the suspected involvement of a financial institution in financial crime.

sections.heading: A person may not subject another person to any prejudice in employment, or
sections.title: Protection from prejudice for whistleblowers
sections.summary: No person may subject another person to employment prejudice or penalties based on the person making a report under section 252 or 253, whether the report was legally required or not.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides legal protection for whistleblowers reporting financial risks, which impacts DTIA by ensuring cross-border data transfers don't face retaliation risks.

requirements.text: No person may subject another person to employment prejudice for reporting under section 252
No person may subject another person to penalties for reporting under section 253

sections.source_quote: 254. A person may not subject another person to any prejudice in employment, or penalise another person in any way, on the ground that the other person— (a) made a report in terms of section 252; or (b) made a report in terms of section 253, even if the report was not required by law.

sections.heading: Sections 252 and 253 apply in addition to, and do not limit, any other law that
sections.title: Reporting protections apply alongside other laws
sections.summary: Sections 252 and 253 provide additional protections for whistleblowers that do not limit or override other laws offering similar protections.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures whistleblower protections are additive to existing legal frameworks, which is relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border reporting obligations.

requirements.text: Sections 252 and 253 apply in addition to other laws providing protection for whistleblowers

sections.source_quote: 255. Sections 252 and 253 apply in addition to, and do not limit, any other law that provides protection for persons who properly report contraventions of the law.

sections.heading: The purpose of the Register is to provide reliable access to accurate, authoritative
sections.title: Purpose of Financial Sector Information Register
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Information Register aims to provide reliable, accurate, and up-to-date information on financial sector laws, regulations, regulatory instruments, and their implementation for stakeholders.
sections.dtia_summary: Serves as a centralized repository for financial regulations, which impacts DTIA by ensuring compliance with cross-border regulatory requirements.

requirements.text: Register provides reliable access to accurate, authoritative and up to date information

sections.source_quote: 257. The purpose of the Register is to provide reliable access to accurate, authoritative and up to date information relating to financial sector laws, Regulations, regulatory instruments and their implementation.

sections.heading: (1) The Register is, for all purposes, taken to be a complete and accurate record
sections.title: Register corrections for financial entities
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Director-General may correct errors in the Register. If corrections are made, the Director-General must annotate records to explain the rectification, specify the date and time of correction, and state the reason for the correction. The Register is a public record maintained by the Director-General for financial entities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures transparency and accuracy in the Register, which is critical for DTIAs as it governs the correction of records related to financial entities' compliance status.
