sections.heading: (1) The Director-General may arrange for the Register to be corrected to rectify
sections.title: Register correction procedures
sections.summary: This section requires that if the Register is corrected, the Director-General must annotate relevant records to explain the nature of the rectification, specify the date and time of correction, and state the reason for the rectification. The Register is a public record maintained by the Director-General for financial entities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures transparency and accuracy in the Register, which is critical for DTIAs as it governs the correction of records related to financial entities' compliance status.

requirements.text: The Director-General may arrange for the Register to be corrected to rectify errors

The Director-General must annotate relevant records in the Register to explain the nature of the rectification and specify the date and time the rectification was made and the reason for the rectification

sections.source_quote: 263. (1) The Director-General may arrange for the Register to be corrected to rectify errors. (2) If the Register is corrected, the Director-General must annotate relevant records in the Register to explain the nature of the rectification and specify the date and time the rectification was made and the reason for the rectification.

sections.heading: (1) A supervised entity that contravenes section 131(1)(b) commits an offence
sections.title: Offences for contravening financial regulations
sections.summary: This section outlines offences for financial entities or individuals who contravene specific sections of the Act. Penalties include fines up to R1,000 per day for contravening section 131(1)(b), R5,000,000 for contravening section 132(4)(a)(iii), R2,500,000 for removing business documents contrary to directives, and R1,000,000 or imprisonment for contravening section 133 or 139.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes penalties for non-compliance with financial regulations, which is relevant for DTIAs as it impacts the legal consequences of data handling practices across borders.

requirements.text: A supervised entity that contravenes section 131(1)(b) commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R1 000 for each day during which the offence continues
A supervised entity that or person who contravenes section 132(4)(a)(iii) commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R5 000 000

If a financial sector regulator gives a directive and without reasonable excuse a business document is removed, concealed, destroyed or interfered with, the supervised entity or person commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R2 500 000
A person who contravenes section 133 commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R1 000 000 or imprisonment for a period not exceeding 12 months, or to both
A person who contravenes section 139 commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R5 000 000 or imprisonment for a period not exceeding two years, or to both

sections.source_quote: 267. (1) A supervised entity that contravenes section 131(1)(b) commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R1 000 for each day during which the offence continues. (2) A supervised entity that or person who contravenes section 132(4)(a)(iii) commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R5 000 00. (3) If— (a) a financial sector regulator gives a directive in terms of section 132(4)(a)(iii); and (b) without reasonable excuse, a business document to which the directive relates is removed from the premises, or concealed, destroyed or otherwise interfered with, contrary to the directive, the supervised entity or person on whom the directive was served commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R2 500 000. (4) A person who contravenes section 133 commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R1 000 000 or imprisonment for a period not exceeding 12 months, or to both a fine and such imprisonment. (5) A person who contravenes section 139 commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R5 000 000 or imprisonment for a period not exceeding two years, or to both a fine and such imprisonment.

sections.heading: (1) (a) A financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank commits an offence if
sections.title: Unauthorized disclosure of financial information
sections.summary: This section prohibits unauthorized disclosure or sharing of information by financial sector regulators or the Reserve Bank. Unauthorized sharing includes information not authorized under section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner not authorized under section 251(5). Both the person sharing information and the regulator or Reserve Bank are liable for offenses.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is critical for DTIAs as it governs the conditions under which financial information can be shared across borders, ensuring compliance with authorization requirements.
sections.authority: Financial sector regulator or Reserve Bank

requirements.text: A financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank commits an offence if information is disclosed or shared for a purpose that is not authorised in terms of section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner that is not authorised as referred to in section 251(5)

Both an official or employee who shares or discloses information, and the financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank on whose behalf the information is shared or disclosed, commit an offence if an official or employee— (i) who is not authorised to share or disclose information shares or discloses information in contravention of section 251(6)(c); (ii) who is authorised to share or disclose information shares or discloses information for a purpose that is not authorised in terms of section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner that contravenes section 251(3) or (4)

sections.source_quote: 272. (1) (a) A financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank commits an offence if information is disclosed or shared for a purpose that is not authorised in terms of section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner that is not authorised as referred to in section 251(5). (b) Both an official or employee who shares or discloses information, and the financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank on whose behalf the information is shared or disclosed, commit an offence if an official or employee— (i) who is not authorised to share or disclose information shares or discloses information in contravention of section 251(6)(c); (ii) who is authorised to share or disclose information shares or discloses information for a purpose that is not authorised in terms of section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner that contravenes section 251(3) or (4).

sections.heading: (a) Bolaodi jwa lephata la ditsˇhelete kgotsa Banka ya Resefe e tlola molao fa
sections.title: Unauthorized disclosure of financial information
sections.summary: This section prohibits unauthorized disclosure or sharing of information by financial sector regulators or the Reserve Bank. Unauthorized sharing includes information not authorized under section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner not authorized under section 251(5). Both the person sharing information and the regulator or Reserve Bank are liable for offenses.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is critical for DTIAs as it governs the conditions under which financial information can be shared across borders, ensuring compliance with authorization requirements.
sections.authority: Financial sector regulator or Reserve Bank

requirements.text: A financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank commits an offence if information is disclosed or shared for a purpose that is not authorised in terms of section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner that is not authorised as referred to in section 251(5)

Both an official or employee who shares or discloses information, and the financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank on whose behalf the information is shared or disclosed, commit an offence if an official or employee— (i) who is not authorised to share or disclose information shares or discloses information in contravention of section 251(6)(c); (ii) who is authorised to share or disclose information shares or discloses information for a purpose that is not authorised in terms of section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner that contravenes section 251(3) or (4)

sections.source_quote: 272. (1) (a) A financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank commits an offence if information is disclosed or shared for a purpose that is not authorised in terms of section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner that is not authorised as referred to in section 251(5). (b) Both an official or employee who shares or discloses information, and the financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank on whose behalf the information is shared or disclosed, commit an offence if an official or employee— (i) who is not authorised to share or disclose information shares or discloses information in contravention of section 251(6)(c); (ii) who is authorised to share or disclose information shares or discloses information for a purpose that is not authorised in terms of section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner that contravenes section 251(3) or (4).

sections.heading: A person who provides to a financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank,
sections.title: False or misleading information to regulators
sections.summary: This section prohibits providing false or misleading information to financial sector regulators or the Reserve Bank, including by omission. The person providing the information commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R10,000,000 or imprisonment for up to 10 years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it addresses the integrity of information shared with regulators, which impacts data accuracy and compliance across borders.

requirements.text: A person who provides to a financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank, information in connection with the operation of a financial sector law, that the person knew or believed, or ought reasonably to have known or believed, to be false or misleading, including by omission, commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R10 000 000 or imprisonment for a period not exceeding 10 years, or to both a fine and such imprisonment

sections.source_quote: 273. A person who provides to a financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank, information in connection with the operation of a financial sector law, that the person knew or believed, or ought reasonably to have known or believed, to be false or misleading, including by omission, commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R10 000 000 or imprisonment for a period not exceeding 10 years, or to both a fine and such imprisonment.

sections.heading: A person who is required in terms of a financial sector law to keep accounts or
sections.title: Accounting inaccuracies and intentional deception in financial records
sections.summary: A person required by financial sector law to maintain accounts or records commits an offence if the records inaccurately reflect transactions or operations, and the person knew or recklessly disregarded the inaccuracy, intended to deceive a regulator, or intended to hinder regulatory investigations. Conviction may result in a fine up to R10 million or imprisonment for up to 10 years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes strict accountability for inaccurate financial records, requiring precise documentation to prevent regulatory evasion and ensure transparency in financial operations across borders.

requirements.text: Accounts or records must correctly record and explain matters, transactions, acts or operations to which they relate
Person must have known or been reckless about whether accounts or records correctly recorded and explained matters, transactions, acts or operations
Person must have intended to deceive or mislead a financial sector regulator or investigator
Person must have intended to hinder or obstruct a financial sector regulator or investigator in performing duties

sections.source_quote: 274. A person who is required in terms of a financial sector law to keep accounts or records commits an offence if— (a) the accounts or records do not correctly record and explain the matters, transactions, acts or operations to which they relate; and (b) the person— (i) knew that, or was reckless whether, the accounts or records correctly recorded and explained the matters, transactions, acts or operations to which they relate; (ii) intended to deceive or mislead a financial sector regulator or an investigator; or (iii) intended to hinder or obstruct a financial sector regulator, or an investigator in performing his or her duties in terms of a financial sector law, and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R10 000 000, or imprisonment for a period not exceeding 10 years, or to both a fine and such imprisonment.

sections.heading: A person who, without the consent of the financial sector regulator, applies to a
sections.title: Unauthorized use of financial regulator names in business operations
sections.summary: A person who applies to a company, body, business or undertaking a name or description implying a connection with a financial sector regulator without the regulator's consent commits an offence. Conviction may result in a fine up to R5 million.
sections.dtia_summary: This section prevents misleading representations that could confuse financial regulators with third parties, ensuring clear distinction between regulated entities and regulatory bodies in cross-border financial operations.

requirements.text: A person may not apply to a company, body, business or undertaking a name or description that reasonably signifies or implies some connection between the company, body, business or undertaking and a financial sector regulator without the consent of the financial sector regulator

sections.source_quote: 275. A person who, without the consent of the financial sector regulator, applies to a company, body, business or undertaking a name or description that reasonably signifies or implies some connection between the company, body, business or undertaking and a financial sector regulator commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R5 000 000.

sections.heading: A financial sector regulator must, if asked, assist a person to make a complaint
sections.title: Regulator assistance in financial complaints
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators must assist individuals in making complaints to the appropriate ombud regarding financial products or services when a person's actions or practices violate financial sector law. This includes facilitating complaints against entities providing financial services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables cross-border complaint mechanisms by ensuring regulators support individuals in reporting violations, which is critical for international financial dispute resolution.

requirements.text: Financial sector regulator must assist a person to make a complaint to the appropriate ombud about actions or practices in terms of a financial sector law of a person in connection with providing financial products or financial services

sections.source_quote: 277. A financial sector regulator must, if asked, assist a person to make a complaint to the appropriate ombud about the actions or practices in terms of a financial sector law, of a person in connection with providing financial products or financial services.

sections.heading: A person, including a financial sector regulator, who suffers loss because of a
sections.title: Recovery of losses from contraventions
sections.summary: A person, including financial sector regulators, who suffers loss due to a contravention of financial sector law by another person may recover the loss through legal action against the contravening person or any knowingly involved party. This applies to both individuals and regulators.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows for cross-border recovery of financial losses by ensuring legal recourse against entities that violate financial regulations, which is vital for international financial accountability.

requirements.text: A person who suffers loss because of a contravention of a financial sector law by another person may recover the amount of the loss by action in a court of competent jurisdiction against the other person

A person who suffers loss because of a contravention of a financial sector law by another person may recover the amount of the loss by action in a court of competent jurisdiction against any person who was knowingly involved in the contravention

sections.source_quote: 278. A person, including a financial sector regulator, who suffers loss because of a contravention of a financial sector law by another person, may recover the amount of the loss by action in a court of competent jurisdiction against— (a) the other person; and (b) any person who was knowingly involved in the contravention.

sections.heading: (1) A financial sector regulator may, for a valid reason, extend any period for
sections.title: Extensions for compliance periods
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators may extend compliance periods for financial sector laws, except for provisions they must comply with, for valid reasons. Extensions can be granted multiple times, either before or after the original deadline.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides flexibility for cross-border compliance with financial regulations, allowing extended timelines for entities operating internationally without compromising regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: Financial sector regulator may extend any period for compliance with, or a period prescribed by, a provision of a financial sector law, other than a provision that the financial sector regulator must comply with, for a valid reason
Financial sector regulator may grant an extension in terms of subsection (1) more than once
Financial sector regulator may grant an extension in terms of subsection (1) either before or after the time for compliance has passed or the period prescribed has ended

sections.source_quote: 279. (1) A financial sector regulator may, for a valid reason, extend any period for compliance with, or a period prescribed by, a provision of a financial sector law, other than a provision that the financial sector regulator must comply with. (2) A financial sector regulator may grant an extension in terms of subsection (1) more than once, and may do so either before or after the time for compliance has passed or the period prescribed has ended.

sections.heading: (1) A licence may be given subject to conditions specified in the licence or in the
sections.title: Conditions for financial licences
sections.summary: Licences for financial activities may be granted subject to conditions specified in the licence or the notice of grant. Suspension, cancellation, or revocation of licences may also be subject to conditions in the notice given to the licensee. Contravention of conditions does not affect the licence status.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that cross-border financial entities operate under clear conditions, which is essential for maintaining regulatory alignment and accountability in international financial operations.

requirements.text: A licence may be given subject to conditions specified in the licence or in the notice of the grant or issue of the licence given to the licensee

A suspension, cancellation or revocation of a licence in terms of a financial sector law may be subject to conditions specified in the notice of the suspension, cancellation or revocation given to the licensee
Contravention of a condition in terms of subsection (2) does not affect the suspension, cancellation or revocation of the licence

sections.source_quote: 280. (1) A licence may be given subject to conditions specified in the licence or in the notice of the grant or issue of the licence given to the licensee. (2) A suspension, cancellation or revocation of a licence in terms of a financial sector law may be subject to conditions specified in the notice of the suspension, cancellation or revocation given to the licensee. (3) Contravention of a condition in terms of subsection (2) does not affect the suspension, cancellation or revocation of the licence.

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority for a financial sector law may, in writing and with
sections.title: Exemptions from financial sector laws with public interest and regulatory objectives
sections.summary: The responsible authority for a financial sector law may exempt persons or classes from specified provisions unless it determines that granting the exemption would be contrary to public interest or prejudice the achievement of the financial sector law's objectives. This exemption power applies only when the financial sector law lacks a specific exemption mechanism. The authority must grant exemptions in accordance with the law and publish them.
sections.dtia_summary: Exemptions require assessment of public interest and regulatory objectives before granting, impacting cross-border data transfers if financial institutions seek exemptions for international data processing activities.
sections.authority: Responsible authority for a financial sector law

requirements.text: The responsible authority may exempt any person or class of persons from a specified provision of the financial sector law
The responsible authority must consider that granting the exemption will be contrary to the public interest
The responsible authority must consider that granting the exemption may prejudice the achievement of the objects of a financial sector law
The exemption applies only if the financial sector law does not provide a power to grant exemptions
The responsible authority must grant the exemption in terms of the relevant provisions of the financial sector law
The responsible authority must publish each exemption

sections.source_quote: 281. (1) The responsible authority for a financial sector law may, in writing and with the concurrence of the other financial sector regulator, exempt any person or class of persons from a specified provision of the financial sector law, unless it considers that granting the exemption— (a) will be contrary to the public interest; or (b) may prejudice the achievement of the objects of a financial sector law.

sections.heading: (1) Bothati jo rwalang maikarabelo a molao wa lephata la ditsˇhelete bo ka, ka go

This exemption power applies only when the financial sector law lacks a specific exemption mechanism. The authority must grant exemptions in accordance with the law and publish them.
sections.dtia_summary: Exemptions require assessment of public interest and regulatory objectives before granting, impacting cross-border data transfers if financial institutions seek exemptions for international data processing activities.

The exemption applies only if the financial sector law does not provide a power to grant exemptions
The responsible authority must grant the exemption in terms of the relevant provisions of the financial sector law
The responsible authority must publish each exemption

sections.source_quote: 281. sections.heading: (1) If this Act provides that a financial sector regulator must notify the other
sections.title: Exemption and concurrence notifications via memoranda of understanding
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators may avoid notifying or seeking concurrence from other regulators if they have a memorandum of understanding that confirms failure to notify does not prejudice regulatory objectives or that notification is unnecessary. This applies to both notification requirements and concurrence requirements for actions under financial sector laws.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows financial institutions to bypass regulatory notifications for cross-border data transfers if mutual agreements exist with other regulators regarding non-prejudice to objectives.

requirements.text: If a financial sector regulator must notify another regulator of a matter, notification is not required if the other regulator has agreed that failure to provide notice does not prejudice its objective
If a financial sector regulator must notify another regulator of a matter, notification is not required if the other regulator has agreed that the notification is unnecessary
If a financial sector regulator must seek concurrence for an action, concurrence is not required if the other regulator has agreed that the action does not prejudice its objective
If a financial sector regulator must seek concurrence for an action, concurrence is not required if the other regulator has agreed that its concurrence is unnecessary
If a financial sector regulator must seek concurrence from the Reserve Bank, concurrence is not required if the Reserve Bank has agreed that concurrence is unnecessary

sections.source_quote: 282. (1) If this Act provides that a financial sector regulator must notify the other financial sector regulator of a particular matter, the notification is not required if the other regulator has agreed, in a section 77 memorandum of understanding or otherwise, that— (a) failure to provide the notice does not prejudice the achievement of its objective; and (b) the notification is unnecessary.

sections.heading: Each of the financial sector regulators and the Ombud Council must establish
sections.title: Regulators must establish consultation arrangements with financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators and the Ombud Council must establish and implement arrangements to facilitate consultation and information exchange with financial institutions, customers, and prospective customers on matters of mutual interest. This ensures transparency and stakeholder engagement in financial sector operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regulators to maintain communication channels with financial institutions for data sharing and consultation, impacting cross-border data transfers through stakeholder engagement mechanisms.
sections.authority: Financial sector regulators and Ombud Council

requirements.text: Each financial sector regulator and the Ombud Council must establish arrangements to facilitate consultation

Each financial sector regulator and the Ombud Council must give effect to arrangements for exchange of information with financial institutions, financial customers, and prospective financial customers

sections.source_quote: 283. Each of the financial sector regulators and the Ombud Council must establish and give effect to arrangements to facilitate consultation and the exchange of information with financial institutions, financial customers, and prospective financial customers on matters of mutual interest.

sections.heading: In any proceedings in terms of, or in relation to, a financial sector law, the records
sections.title: Financial institution records admissible as evidence in proceedings
sections.summary: Records and books of account of financial institutions and their control function employees are admissible as evidence in proceedings related to financial sector laws. This ensures transparency and accountability in financial transactions and regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institution records are admissible as evidence in cross-border data transfers, impacting data retention and evidence collection for international financial disputes.

requirements.text: Records and books of account of a financial institution are admissible as evidence in proceedings in terms of or in relation to a financial sector law

Records and books of account of a person engaged by a financial institution to perform a control function are admissible as evidence in proceedings in terms of or in relation to a financial sector law

sections.source_quote: 284. In any proceedings in terms of, or in relation to, a financial sector law, the records and books of account of a financial institution, and of a person who is engaged by a financial institution to perform a control function, are admissible as evidence of the matters, transactions and accounts recorded therein.

sections.heading: The State, the Minister, the Reserve Bank, the Governor and Deputy Governors,
sections.title: Immunities for financial sector officials and regulators
sections.summary: State officials, regulators, Reserve Bank members, and financial sector regulators are immune from liability for losses or damage incurred from decisions or actions taken in good faith while exercising their functions under financial sector laws. This protects officials from legal consequences of regulatory actions.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides immunity for financial regulators involved in cross-border data transfers, reducing legal risks for institutions engaging in international financial activities.

requirements.text: The State, the Minister, the Reserve Bank, the Governor and Deputy Governors, a financial sector regulator, and related officials are not liable for loss or damage from decisions taken in good faith in the exercise of functions under financial sector laws

sections.source_quote: 285. The State, the Minister, the Reserve Bank, the Governor and Deputy Governors, a financial sector regulator, a member of the Executive Committee, a member of the Prudential Committee, a member of a subcommittee of the Prudential Authority or the Financial Sector Conduct Authority, a member of the Tribunal, the Ombud Council, a member of the Ombud Board, an employee of the State, a board member or officer of the Reserve Bank, a staff member of a financial sector regulator, a staff member of the Reserve Bank, a person appointed by a financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank to exercise a power or perform a function or duty in terms of a financial sector law is not liable for, or in respect of, any loss or damage suffered or incurred by any person arising from a decision taken or action performed in good faith in the exercise of a function, power or duty in terms of a financial sector law.

sections.heading: (1) The Minister may make Regulations to facilitate the implementation of this
sections.title: Regulation publication and consultation process for financial sector laws
sections.summary: The Minister may issue regulations to implement the Act, including those prescribing requirements or addressing procedural matters. Regulations must be published in the Gazette and Register, with a draft, explanation, impact statement, and submission invitation. The Minister must consult Parliament on regulations, submit a consultation report, and ensure submissions are considered before finalizing regulations. The process requires at least six weeks for submissions and 30 days for parliamentary scrutiny.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates a transparent regulatory process for financial sector regulations, requiring public consultation and parliamentary review before implementation. It ensures that regulations are well-considered and aligned with stakeholder interests, which is critical for data transfer impact assessments involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: The Minister may make Regulations to facilitate implementation of this Act
Regulations must be published in the Gazette and Register
Draft regulations must include explanation of need and intended operation
Regulations must include impact statement
Regulations must invite submissions with clear submission details
Minister must submit consultation report to Parliament
Submissions period must be at least six weeks
Parliamentary scrutiny period must be at least 30 days
Regulations must be repeated if materially different from draft
Minister must publish draft and explanation if likely to cause prejudice

sections.source_quote: 288. (1) The Minister may make Regulations to facilitate the implementation of this Act, including Regulations—(a) that must or may be prescribed in terms of this Act; (b) to provide for other procedural or administrative matters that are necessary to implement the provisions of this Act.

sections.heading: (1) Tona o ka dira Melawana go nolofatsa go tsenngwa mo tirisong ga Molao

sections.heading: (1) The functions of the Prudential Authority in relation to medical schemes and
sections.title: Council for Medical Schemes replaces Prudential Authority functions
sections.summary: The Council for Medical Schemes can exercise the Prudential Authority's functions and the Financial Sector Conduct Authority's functions related to medical schemes instead of those regulators, with the concurrence of the respective authorities. Any determination must be published, and concurrence is not required if the regulator agrees in writing that the function does not prejudice its objectives.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the Council for Medical Schemes to handle regulatory functions for medical schemes, which may impact data transfer assessments involving health-related financial data.

requirements.text: Council for Medical Schemes exercises Prudential Authority functions instead of the Authority
Council for Medical Schemes exercises Financial Sector Conduct Authority functions instead of the Authority
Determination must be published
Concurrence of regulator not required if regulator agrees in writing that function does not prejudice objectives

sections.source_quote: 291. (1) The functions of the Prudential Authority in relation to medical schemes and the associated powers and duties of the Prudential Authority are, to the extent determined by, and subject to any conditions determined by, the Minister, to be exercised by the Council for Medical Schemes instead of the Prud. Auth.

sections.heading: (1) This section applies for the period of three years from the date on which this
sections.title: Financial Sector Conduct Authority assumes prudential powers
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Conduct Authority takes over the Prudential Authority's power to make prudential standards for collective investment schemes, pension funds, and friendly societies. These standards must align with specific financial sector laws and do not affect the Financial Sector Conduct Authority's other powers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section transfers prudential oversight responsibilities to the Financial Sector Conduct Authority, which may impact data transfer assessments for financial institutions.

requirements.text: Financial Sector Conduct Authority exercises prudential standards for collective investment schemes
Financial Sector Conduct Authority exercises prudential standards for pension funds
Financial Sector Conduct Authority exercises prudential standards for friendly societies
Prudential standards must comply with specific financial sector laws

sections.source_quote: 292. (2) The power of the Prudential Authority to make prudential standards... is to be exercised by the Financial Sector Conduct Authority: (a) Collective investment schemes as defined in section 1(1) of the Collective Investment Schemes Control Act, 2002; (b) pension funds as defined in section 1(1) of the Pension Funds Act; (c) friendly societies as defined in section 1(1) of the Friendly Societies Act.

sections.heading: (1) At the date on which this section comes into effect, the assets and liabilities
sections.title: Assets and liabilities transfer from Financial Services Board
sections.summary: The Financial Services Board's assets and liabilities transfer to the Financial Sector Conduct Authority without conveyance or assignment. Entities maintaining databases must record this transfer. The transfer does not create tax or duty liabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures a smooth transfer of assets and liabilities between regulatory bodies, which may impact data transfer assessments involving financial records.

requirements.text: Assets and liabilities of Financial Services Board become liabilities of Financial Sector Conduct Authority
Entities maintaining databases must record the transfer
Transfer does not create tax or duty liabilities

sections.source_quote: 293. (1) At the date on which this section comes into effect, the assets and liabilities of the Financial Services Board cease to be assets and liabilities of the Board and become assets and liabilities of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority without any conveyance, transfer or assignment.

sections.heading: (1) Ka letlha leo karolo eno e tsenngwang mo tirisong ka lona, dithoto le melato
sections.title: Assets and liabilities transfer from Financial Services Board
sections.summary: The Financial Services Board's assets and liabilities transfer to the Financial Sector Conduct Authority without conveyance or assignment. Entities maintaining databases must record this transfer. The transfer does not create tax or duty liabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures a smooth transfer of assets and liabilities between regulatory bodies, which may impact data transfer assessments involving financial records.

requirements.text: Assets and liabilities of Financial Services Board become liabilities of Financial Sector Conduct Authority
Entities maintaining databases must record the transfer
Transfer does not create tax or duty liabilities

sections.source_quote: 293. (1) At the date on which this section comes into effect, the assets and liabilities of the Financial Services Board cease to be assets and liabilities of the Board and become assets and liabilities of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority without any conveyance, transfer or assignment.

sections.heading: (1) (a) At the date on which this section comes into effect, the staff of the
sections.title: Staff transfer from Financial Services Board to regulators
sections.summary: This section mandates the transfer of Financial Services Board staff to the Financial Sector Conduct Authority and South African Reserve Bank under the Labour Relations Act. It clarifies that 'old employer' refers to the Financial Services Board and 'new employer' refers to the two regulators. It requires pension fund agreements to address staff transfers to the South African Reserve Bank's pension fund and ensures tax treatment continuity for retired staff.

requirements.text: Staff of the Financial Services Board must be transferred to Financial Sector Conduct Authority and South African Reserve Bank under Labour Relations Act
Old employer in Labour Relations Act refers to Financial Services Board
New employer in Labour Relations Act refers to Financial Sector Conduct Authority or South African Reserve Bank
Agreements must address transfer of staff to South African Reserve Bank pension fund
Retired staff from Financial Services Board must maintain same tax treatment as before
Pension fund of Financial Services Board becomes pension fund of Financial Sector Conduct Authority

sections.source_quote: 294. (1) (a) At the date on which this section comes into effect, the staff of the Financial Services Board must be transferred to the Financial Sector Conduct Authority and the South African Reserve Bank, respectively, in accordance with section 197 of the Labour Relations Act, 1995.

sections.heading: (1) The PrudentialAuthority must prepare each annual report of a financial sector
sections.title: Annual reports for financial sector regulators
sections.summary: This section requires the Prudential Authority to prepare annual reports for financial sector regulators mandated by financial sector laws. It mandates the Financial Sector Conduct Authority to prepare annual reports for the Financial Services Board and other financial sector regulators. Reports may be published as part of the first annual report of either authority.

requirements.text: Prudential Authority must prepare annual reports for financial sector regulators
Financial Sector Conduct Authority must prepare annual reports for Financial Services Board and other financial sector regulators
Reports may be published as part of first annual report of Prudential Authority or Financial Sector Conduct Authority

sections.source_quote: 295. (1) The Prudential Authority must prepare each annual report of a financial sector regulator required by a financial sector law for which it is the responsible authority, for the reporting period during which this section comes into effect.

sections.heading: (1) An inspection or investigation in terms of the Banks Act, the Reserve Bank
sections.title: Continuation of pending inspections and investigations
sections.summary: This section allows continuation and conclusion of pending inspections or investigations under various financial sector laws by the Prudential Authority or Financial Sector Conduct Authority. It covers inspections from the Banks Act, Reserve Bank Act, Mutual Banks Act, Co-operative Banks Act, Short-term Insurance Act, and Long-term Insurance Act. It also addresses inspections under Financial Services Board legislation.

requirements.text: Pending inspections under Banks Act, Reserve Bank Act, Mutual Banks Act, Co-operative Banks Act, Short-term Insurance Act, Long-term Insurance Act may be continued by Prudential Authority
Pending inspections under Financial Services Board legislation may be continued by Financial Sector Conduct Authority

sections.source_quote: 296. (1) An inspection or investigation in terms of the Banks Act, the Reserve Bank Act, the Mutual Banks Act, 1993, the Co-operative Banks Act, 2007, the Short-term Insurance Act or the Long-term Insurance Act that is pending and not concluded immediately before the date on which this section comes into effect may be continued and concluded by the Prudential Authority in terms of the relevant provisions of this Act, or by the Financial Sector Conduct Authority in relation to an inspection or investigation in terms of the Short-term Insurance Act or the Long-term Insurance Act.

sections.heading: An arrangement in terms of a financial sector law between a registrar, supervisor
sections.title: Continuation of foreign government agency arrangements
sections.summary: This section states that arrangements between financial sector regulators and foreign government agencies in force on the date of this section's effect continue as with the substitution of the relevant financial sector regulator. These arrangements may be amended or terminated according to their terms.

requirements.text: Arrangements between financial sector regulators and foreign government agencies continue in effect with relevant regulator substitution
Arrangements may be amended or terminated in accordance with their terms

sections.source_quote: 297. An arrangement in terms of a financial sector law between a registrar, supervisor or other financial sector regulator and a foreign government agency that is in force on the date on which this section comes into effect continues in effect as with the substitution of the relevant financial sector regulator for the registrar, supervisor or the other financial sector regulator, but may be amended or terminated in accordance with the terms of the arrangement.

sections.heading: (1) (a) Despite the repeals effected in the terms of this Part—
sections.title: Continuation of Enforcement Committee and Appeal Board proceedings
sections.summary: This section ensures that the Enforcement Committee and Appeal Board panels continue to handle matters they were dealing with immediately before the section's effective date. It requires the Financial Sector Conduct Authority to provide administrative support to these bodies and specifies when proceedings are instituted for each committee.

requirements.text: Enforcement Committee continues to deal with matters it was handling immediately before effective date
Appeal Board panels continue to deal with matters it was handling immediately before effective date
Enforcement Committee and panels continue in existence for purposes of this section only
Financial Sector Conduct Authority must provide administrative and other support to Enforcement Committee and panels

sections.source_quote: 298. (1) (a) Despite the repeals effected in the terms of this Part— (i) the Enforcement Committee is to continue to deal with any matter that it was dealing with immediately before the date on which this Part comes into effect; and (ii) a panel of the Appeal Board is to continue to deal with any matter that it was dealing with immediately before that date.

sections.heading: (1) Tlhatlhobo kgotsa patlisiso go ya ka Banks Act, Mutual Banks Act, 1993
sections.title: Continuation of pending inspections and investigations
sections.summary: This section allows continuation and conclusion of pending inspections or investigations under various financial sector laws by the Prudential Authority or Financial Sector Conduct Authority. It covers inspections from the Banks Act, Reserve Bank Act, Mutual Banks Act, Co-operative Banks Act, Short-term Insurance Act, and Long-term Insurance Act. It also addresses inspections under Financial Services Board legislation.

requirements.text: Pending inspections under Banks Act, Reserve Bank Act, Mutual Banks Act, Co-operative Banks Act, Short-term Insurance Act, Long-term Insurance Act may be continued by Prudential Authority
Pending inspections under Financial Services Board legislation may be continued by Financial Sector Conduct Authority

sections.source_quote: 296. sections.heading: (1) Go sa nyatswe phimolo ya karolo 9 ya Banks Act go ya ka Sˇejule 4, kopo ya
sections.title: Substitution of parties in pending proceedings
sections.summary: This section requires the Prudential Authority to be substituted as a party in pending proceedings for the Reserve Bank or a registrar under specified financial sector laws when the section comes into effect. Similarly, the Financial Sector Conduct Authority must be substituted as a party for the Financial Services Board or relevant registrars. These substitutions apply to all pending proceedings that have not been finally determined.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by requiring substitution of regulatory bodies in pending proceedings, which may impact cross-border data transfers if the proceedings involve international entities or data flows.
sections.authority: Prudential Authority, Financial Sector Conduct Authority

requirements.text: The Prudential Authority must be substituted as a party in any pending proceedings that have been commenced but not finally determined immediately before the date on which this section comes into effect for the Reserve Bank or a registrar in terms of the Banks Act, the Mutual Banks Act, 1993, the Co-operative Banks Act, 2007, the Short-term Insurance Act or the Long-term Insurance Act.

The Financial Sector Conduct Authority must be substituted as a party in any pending proceedings that have been commenced but not finally determined immediately before the date on which this section comes into effect for the Financial Services Board, the Directorate of Market Abuse where applicable, or a registrar in terms of a financial sector law other than the Banks Act.

sections.source_quote: 300. (1) Go sa nyatswe phimolo ya karolo 9 ya Banks Act go ya ka Sˇejule 4, kopo ya tshekatsheko e e dirilweng go ya ka karolo eo mme e ise e tlhomamiswe ka konotelelo pele ga letlha leo karolo eno e tsenngwang mo tirisong ka lona e ka tsweletswa fa pele ga boto ya tshekatsheko, eo e tshwanetseng go diragatsa dithata tsa Lekgotla mabapi le kopo.

sections.heading: (1) A licence, authorisation, approval, registration, consent or similar permission
sections.title: Continuation of licenses and regulatory instruments
sections.summary: This section states that licenses, authorizations, approvals, registrations, consents, and similar permissions given under financial sector laws remain in force but may be amended or revoked by the responsible authority. It also covers rules and regulatory instruments that continue in force with the possibility of amendment or revocation by the relevant authority, including specific provisions for consultations and ombud schemes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it addresses the continuity of regulatory instruments and licenses, which may impact data transfer mechanisms and cross-border data flows within financial institutions.
sections.authority: Responsible authorities for financial sector laws

requirements.text: Licenses, authorizations, approvals, registrations, consents, and similar permissions given under financial sector laws remain in force immediately before the date on which this section comes into effect but may be amended or revoked by the responsible authority for the financial sector law.

Rules made in terms of section 26 of the Financial Advisory and Intermediary Services Act and in force immediately before the date on which this section comes into effect have effect as Ombud Council rules and may be amended or revoked by Ombud Council rules.

Regulatory instruments or regulations made under financial sector laws and in force immediately before the date on which this section comes into effect remain in force but may be amended or revoked by a regulatory instrument made by the responsible authority.

Consultations undertaken before the date on which Part 1 of Chapter 7 comes into effect in relation to a regulatory instrument proposed to be made under a specific financial sector law or a proposed financial sector law after that Part came into effect are taken to meet the requirements of this Act for consultation if they meet the requirements of the specific financial sector law for consultation or substantially meet the requirements of this Act for consultation.

Regulations made in terms of section 5 of the Financial Supervision of the Road Accident Fund Act, 1993 continue in force but may be amended or repealed by Regulations made in terms of section 5 by the Prudential Authority.

An ombud scheme that came into effect immediately before the repeal of the Financial Services Ombuds Schemes Act, 2004 and was recognised in terms of that Act must be taken to be a recognised industry ombud scheme as if it had been recognised under this Act.

Subsection (6) ceases to have effect at the end of 12 months after Chapter 14 takes effect, but the Ombud Council may extend the application of that subsection in a particular case for a further period of not more than 6 months on application and for good reason.

sections.source_quote: 301. (1) A licence, authorisation, approval, registration, consent or similar permission given in terms of a financial sector law and in force immediately before the date on which this section comes into effect remains in force for the purposes of the financial sector law, but may be amended or revoked by the responsible authority for the financial sector law, in accordance with the provisions of that financial sector law.

sections.heading: (1) In order to facilitate the coming into effect, appropriate implementation and
sections.title: Transitional arrangements for financial sector regulators
sections.summary: This section allows the Minister to make regulations providing for transitional arrangements regarding the exercise of powers, performance of functions, and duties related to the establishment of financial sector regulators, the coming into operation of different provisions of the Act, and the repeal or amendment of different provisions of laws repealed or amended by this Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it enables transitional arrangements for financial sector regulators, which may impact data transfer mechanisms and cross-border data flows during regulatory transitions.

requirements.text: The Minister may make Regulations providing for transitional arrangements regarding the exercise of powers, the performance of functions and duties, and other matters that may be necessary in relation to the establishment of financial sector regulators and other bodies.

Regulations may provide for the Reserve Bank to exercise specified powers and perform specified functions and duties of the Prudential Authority should it be necessary for powers and functions of the Prudential Authority to be exercised prior to formal establishment.

Regulations may provide for the Financial Services Board to exercise specified powers and perform specified functions and duties of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority should it be necessary for the powers and functions of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority to be exercised prior to formal establishment.

sections.source_quote: 304. (1) In order to facilitate the coming into effect, appropriate implementation and operation of this Act, the Minister may make Regulations providing for transitional arrangements regarding the exercise of powers, the performance of functions and duties, and other matters that may be necessary in relation to— (a) the establishment of the financial sector regulators and other bodies in terms of this Act; (b) the coming into operation of different provisions of this Act; and (c) the repeal or amendment of different provisions of a law repealed or amended by this Act.

sections.heading: (1) Molao ono o bidiwa Molao wa Taolo ya Lephata la Ditsˇhelete, 2017, e bile
sections.title: Recognition of ombud schemes
sections.summary: This section states that ombud schemes that were recognised under the Financial Services Ombuds Schemes Act, 2004 immediately before its repeal must be taken to be recognised industry ombud schemes under this Act, with a provision for a 12-month period after Chapter 14 takes effect, extendable by the Ombud Council for up to 6 months in specific cases.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it addresses the recognition of ombud schemes, which may impact data handling and dispute resolution mechanisms for financial data.

requirements.text: An ombud scheme that came into effect immediately before the repeal of the Financial Services Ombuds Schemes Act, 2.004 and was recognised in terms of that Act must be taken to be a recognised industry ombud scheme as if it had been recognised under this Act.

Subsection (6) ceases to have effect at the end of 12 months after Chapter 14 takes effect, but the Ombud Council may extend the application of that subsection in a particular case for a further period of not more than 6 months on application and for good reason.

sections.source_quote: 305. (1) An ombud scheme that came into effect immediately before the repeal of the Financial Services Ombuds Schemes Act, 2004 and was recognised in terms of that Act must be taken to be a recognised industry ombud scheme as if it had been recognised under this Act.

sections.heading: Regulatory instruments made in terms of financial sector laws
sections.title: Amendment of collective investment scheme regulations
sections.summary: This section amends section 15 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act by modifying the conditions under which the Authority may require actions from managers of collective investment schemes. It specifies that the Authority may act if it considers it in the interests of investors or the public after an on-site visit or inspection, and clarifies the consequences of non-compliance with written requests, directions, or directives from the Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines specific conditions for the Authority to intervene in collective investment schemes, impacting how financial institutions must respond to regulatory actions and maintain compliance with oversight requirements.

requirements.text: The Authority may require actions from managers if it considers it in the interests of investors or the public after an on-site visit or inspection
The Authority may require managers to comply with written requests, directions, or directives

sections.source_quote: 4. The amendment of section 15— (a) by the substitution in subsection (1) for the words preceding paragraph (a) of the following words: '‘If [the registrar, after an on-site visit or inspection under section 14, considers on reasonable grounds that] it is in the interests of the investors of a collective investment scheme or of members of the public [so require], the [registrar] Authority may—'

sections.heading: The amendment of section 83—
sections.title: Financial Sector Regulation Act alignment with other laws
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Financial Sector Regulation Act aligns with the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It requires that any provision of the Act that refers to an inspection must be interpreted as an investigation under the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It also specifies that references to the Authority publishing information online must be interpreted as publishing in the Register, and that prescribed fees must be determined under section 237 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies how the Financial Sector Regulation Act interacts with other regulatory frameworks, particularly regarding inspections, publications, and fees. It ensures consistency in regulatory procedures across different legal instruments.

requirements.text: A reference in this Act to an inspection must be read as a reference to an investigation in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to the Authority publishing information or a document on a web site must be read as a reference to the Authority publishing the information or document in the Register
The Authority may also publish the information or document on its web site
A reference in this Act to a prescribed fee must be read as a reference to the relevant fee determined in terms of section 237 and Chapter 16 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to a review of a decision of the Authority must be read as a reference to a reconsideration of the decision by the Tribunal in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act

sections.source_quote: A reference in this Act to an inspection in terms of a provision of this Act must be read as a reference to an investigation in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act.

sections.heading: The amendment of section 18—
sections.title: Licensing requirements for market infrastructure entities
sections.summary: This section specifies that entities must be licensed as exchanges, central securities depositories, trade repositories, clearing houses, or central counterparties to operate in financial markets. Unlicensed entities cannot purport to be such entities or use names suggesting connections to licensed market infrastructure. The section defines prohibited activities for unlicensed persons to prevent market manipulation and ensure regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes licensing requirements for market infrastructure entities, which affects cross-border data transfers involving financial market participants by restricting unlicensed entities from operating as market infrastructure providers. It requires clear licensing to prevent unauthorized data handling across borders.

requirements.text: A person who is not licensed as an exchange, central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house or central counterparty
A participant, authorised user, clearing member, approved nominee or issuer of listed securities

May not purport to be an exchange, central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house, central counterparty, external central counterparty, external trade repository, participant, authorised user or clearing member
May not behave in a manner or use a name or description suggesting connection to licensed market infrastructure entities

sections.source_quote: A person who is not— (a) licensed as an exchange, a central securities depository, a trade repository [or], a clearing house or a central counterparty; (b) a participant; (c) an authorised user; (d) a clearing member; (e) an approved nominee; [or] (f) an issuer of listed securities, may not purport to be an exchange, central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house, central counterparty, external central counterparty, external trade repository, participant, authorised user, clearing member, approved nominee or issuer of listed securities, as the case may be, or behave in a manner or use a name or description which suggests, signifies or implies that there is some connection between that person and an exchange, a central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house, central counterparty, external central counterparty, external trade repository, participant, authorised user or clearing member, as the case may be, where in fact no such connection exists.

sections.heading: The substitution in section 26 for sub-
sections.title: Supervisory cooperation arrangements for financial market infrastructure
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Financial Sector Regulator must establish supervisory cooperation with equivalent foreign authorities. It requires specific mechanisms for information exchange, breach notification, coordination of inspections, and handling of supervisory information for market infrastructure entities. The obligations apply to the regulator and foreign supervisory authorities overseeing licensed market infrastructure.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires international supervisory cooperation mechanisms for financial market infrastructure, including information exchange protocols and breach notification procedures that impact cross-border data transfers and regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: The Authority must enter into a supervisory co-operation arrangement with the relevant supervisory authority from the equivalent jurisdiction
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify mechanisms for information exchange between the Authority, South African Reserve Bank, Prudential Authority, and relevant supervisory authorities
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify mechanisms for prompt notification to the Authority when a supervisory authority deems an external market infrastructure in breach of conditions
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify procedures for coordination of supervisory activities including cross-border on-site inspections
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify processes for using supervisory information for law enforcement purposes
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must include procedures for cooperation on examination reports and document analysis
The Authority and supervisory authorities must establish confidential safeguards for non-public supervisory information
The Authority and supervisory authorities must share risk analysis assessments to identify and mitigate market risks
The Authority and supervisory authorities must share information on entities of systemic significance
The Authority and supervisory authorities must cooperate in routine oversight of internationally active licensed market infrastructure
The Authority must provide advance notification regarding issues that may affect regulatory interests of other authorities
The Authority must design mechanisms for supervisory cooperation during crisis periods
The Authority and supervisory authorities must maintain ongoing staff communications regarding international market infrastructure

sections.source_quote: 6C. (1) The Authority must enter into a supervisory co-operation arrangement with the relevant supervisory authority from the equivalent jurisdiction for the purpose of performing its functions in terms of this Act.

sections.heading: The substitution in section 30T for
sections.title: Substitution for section 50 on reporting requirements
sections.summary: This section substitutes section 50 to require cooperative banks, financial institutions, representative bodies, or support organizations to submit specific information or reports to the supervisor authority upon written notice. The reports must be prepared at the expense of the institution, ensuring accountability and transparency.
sections.dtia_summary: Cooperative banks must share specific information with the supervisor, impacting data sharing and reporting across borders.

requirements.text: The supervisor may require cooperative banks to submit information or reports upon written notice
Reports must be prepared at the expense of the institution

sections.source_quote: 15. The substitution for section 50 of the following section: '‘Information and reports 50.[(1)] (a) The [supervisor] Authority may on written notice require a co-operative bank, a co-operative financial institution, a representative body or a support organisation [of a co-operative bank] to submit to [him or her] the Authority—'

sections.heading: The amendment of the arrangement
sections.title: Central securities depository operational requirements
sections.summary: This section amends section 29 by requiring that a central securities depository licence specifies the registered office and operational locations. It also mandates that the depository cannot operate at any other location without joint prior written approval from the Registrar Authority, Prudential Authority, and South African Reserve Bank. This applies to central securities depositories operating in South Africa.
sections.authority: Registrar Authority, Prudential Authority, South African Reserve Bank

requirements.text: The licence must specify the registered office of the central securities depository in the Republic and the places where the central securities depository may be operated

The central securities depository may not be operated at any other place without the joint prior written approval of the Registrar Authority, the Prudential Authority and the South African Reserve Bank

sections.source_quote: 19. sections.title: Financial Sector Regulation Act alignment with other laws
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Financial Sector Regulation Act aligns with the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It requires that any provision of the Act that refers to an inspection must be interpreted as an investigation under the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It also specifies that references to the Authority publishing information online must be interpreted as publishing in the Register, and that prescribed fees must be determined under section 237 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies how the Financial Sector Regulation Act interacts with other regulatory frameworks, particularly regarding inspections, publications, and fees. It ensures consistency in regulatory procedures across different legal instruments.

requirements.text: A reference in this Act to an inspection must be read as a reference to an investigation in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to the Authority publishing information or a document on a web site must be read as a reference to the Authority publishing the information or document in the Register
The Authority may also publish the information or document on its web site
A reference in this Act to a prescribed fee must be read as a reference to the relevant fee determined in terms of section 237 and Chapter 16 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to a review of a decision of the Authority must be read as a reference to a reconsideration of the decision by the Tribunal in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act

sections.source_quote: A reference in this Act to an inspection in terms of a provision of this Act must be read as a reference to an investigation in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act.

sections.heading: The substitution in section 33 for sub-
sections.title: Licensing requirements for market infrastructure entities
sections.summary: This section specifies that entities must be licensed as exchanges, central securities depositories, trade repositories, clearing houses, or central counterparties to operate in financial markets. Unlicensed entities cannot purport to be such entities or use names suggesting connections to licensed market infrastructure. The section defines prohibited activities for unlicensed persons to prevent market manipulation and ensure regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes licensing requirements for market infrastructure entities, which affects cross-border data transfers involving financial market participants by restricting unlicensed entities from operating as market infrastructure providers. It requires clear licensing to prevent unauthorized data handling across borders.

requirements.text: A person who is not licensed as an exchange, central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house or central counterparty
A participant, authorised user, clearing member, approved nominee or issuer of listed securities

May not purport to be an exchange, central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house, central counterparty, external central counterparty, external trade repository, participant, authorised user or clearing member
May not behave in a manner or use a name or description suggesting connection to licensed market infrastructure entities

sections.source_quote: A person who is not— (a) licensed as an exchange, a central securities depository, a trade repository [or], a clearing house or a central counterparty; (b) a participant; (c) an authorised user; (d) a clearing member; (e) an approved nominee; [or] (f) an issuer of listed securities, may not purport to be an exchange, central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house, central counterparty, external central counterparty, external trade repository, participant, authorised user, clearing member, approved nominee or issuer of listed securities, as the case may be, or behave in a manner or use a name or description which suggests, signifies or implies that there is some connection between that person and an exchange, a central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house, central counterparty, external central counterparty, external trade repository, participant, authorised user or clearing member, as the case may be, where in fact no such connection exists.

sections.heading: Subartikel (2) van artikel 12 word
sections.title: Amendment of collective investment scheme regulations
sections.summary: This section amends section 15 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act by modifying the conditions under which the Authority may require actions from managers of collective investment schemes. It specifies that the Authority may act if it considers it in the interests of investors or the public after an on-site visit or inspection, and clarifies the consequences of non-compliance with written requests, directions, or directives from the Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines specific conditions for the Authority to intervene in collective investment schemes, impacting how financial institutions must respond to regulatory actions and maintain compliance with oversight requirements.

sections.heading: The amendment of section 23—
sections.title: Supervisory cooperation arrangements for financial market infrastructure
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Financial Sector Regulator must establish supervisory cooperation with equivalent foreign authorities. It requires specific mechanisms for information exchange, breach notification, coordination of inspections, and handling of supervisory information for market infrastructure entities. The obligations apply to the regulator and foreign supervisory authorities overseeing licensed market infrastructure.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires international supervisory cooperation mechanisms for financial market infrastructure, including information exchange protocols and breach notification procedures that impact cross-border data transfers and regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: The Authority must enter into a supervisory co-operation arrangement with the relevant supervisory authority from the equivalent jurisdiction
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify mechanisms for information exchange between the Authority, South African Reserve Bank, Prudential Authority, and relevant supervisory authorities
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify mechanisms for prompt notification to the Authority when a supervisory authority deems an external market infrastructure in breach of conditions
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify procedures for coordination of supervisory activities including cross-border on-site inspections
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify processes for using supervisory information for law enforcement purposes
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must include procedures for cooperation on examination reports and document analysis
The Authority and supervisory authorities must establish confidential safeguards for non-public supervisory information
The Authority and supervisory authorities must share risk analysis assessments to identify and mitigate market risks
The Authority and supervisory authorities must share information on entities of systemic significance
The Authority and supervisory authorities must cooperate in routine oversight of internationally active licensed market infrastructure
The Authority must provide advance notification regarding issues that may affect regulatory interests of other authorities
The Authority must design mechanisms for supervisory cooperation during crisis periods
The Authority and supervisory authorities must maintain ongoing staff communications regarding international market infrastructure

sections.source_quote: 6C. (1) The Authority must enter into a supervisory co-operation arrangement with the relevant supervisory authority from the equivalent jurisdiction for the purpose of performing its functions in terms of this Act.

sections.heading: Artikels 8, 9 en 10 word geskrap.
sections.title: Exemptions for market infrastructure entities
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions for exemptions from licensing requirements for market infrastructure entities. It specifies that exemptions must not conflict with public interest, frustrate the Act's objectives, cause financial hardship, or fail to meet equivalence criteria with foreign jurisdictions. The exemption process requires approval from the Financial Sector Regulation Authority, South African Reserve Bank, and Prudential Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs exemptions for market infrastructure entities, which impacts data transfers by requiring that exemptions do not cause financial hardship or conflict with public interest. It ensures that exempt entities meet equivalence standards for cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: Exemptions must not conflict with public interest
Exemptions must not frustrate the achievement of the objects of this Act
Exemptions must not cause financial or other hardship to applicants or clients
For external market infrastructure, applicant must be based in an equivalent jurisdiction with authorization from supervisory authority
Applicant must comply with joint standards for exemption
Applicant must cooperate and share information with the Authority, South African Reserve Bank and Prudential Authority

sections.source_quote: Exemptions must not— (aa) conflict with the public interest; or (bb) frustrate the achievement of the objects of this Act; and the application of the section will cause the applicant or clients of the applicant financial or other hardship or prejudice.

sections.summary: This section amends section 115 by defining 'fails to comply' as not meeting any direction, requirement, notice, rule, regulatory instrument, or regulation under the Act or Financial Sector Regulation Act. This definition applies to regulatory enforcement actions.
